items.title: Enforce Domain Constraints Before and After Squaring
items.description: When an equation involves square roots, always apply the original nonnegativity constraints and re-check candidates after squaring.
items.content: Square roots require specific expressions to be nonnegative, and squaring can introduce extraneous solutions by losing sign information. After solving the squared equation, substitute each candidate back into the original unsquared equation (or at least verify the required sign condition) to discard invalid roots.
items.reasoning: The correct attempts share a disciplined workflow: (1) introduce an auxiliary expression for the quadratic-inside-the-radical structure, (2) reduce the equation to a simpler algebraic equation in that auxiliary variable, and crucially (3) handle squaring by enforcing the original domain/sign conditions to eliminate extraneous roots. The incorrect attempts fail mainly by mishandling the absolute value/sign that appears after rewriting square roots (e.g., treating √(square) as the square itself without the | | constraint), or by squaring and accepting solutions that violate the original equation’s nonnegativity requirement on the unsquared sides. One incorrect attempt also shows an error from changing the expression being set equal to a new variable (a mismatch in the “what equals what” step), producing a different quadratic and wrong roots.

items.title: Use a Clean Substitution for Repeated Quadratic Forms
items.description: If the equation repeatedly contains the same quadratic expression, define a single auxiliary variable for it to simplify algebra and reduce error risk.
items.content: Introducing an auxiliary variable for the shared “quadratic plus constant” structure turns the problem into a lower-degree equation that is easier to solve and factor. This also improves consistency: you convert the entire equation once, then translate solutions back carefully.

items.title: Handle Absolute Values Correctly for Square Roots of Squares
items.description: Never replace √(something squared) with the squared expression; it equals the absolute value of the underlying quantity.
items.content: Whenever you rewrite a square root of a square (e.g., √(y²)), the result is |y|, which forces casework or sign reasoning. Mishandling this absolute value is a common source of wrong answers when solving equations that become y²−c = 2|y| after simplification.

items.title: Validate the Variable Mapping Back to the Original Equation
items.description: If you redefine variables or complete the square, confirm that each transformed expression corresponds exactly to the original one.
items.content: Errors often come from subtly changing what an auxiliary variable represents (e.g., shifting by a constant incorrectly) so the resulting quadratic solves a different equation. A quick consistency check—re-expressing the original equation in terms of the new variable and verifying each step—prevents this class of mistakes.

items.title: Use Coordinate Setup Aligned to Right Angles
items.description: When a right angle is given, align axes with its legs so the known lengths become direct coordinates.
items.content: Place the right-angle vertex at the origin and put the two endpoints on perpendicular axes using the given segment lengths. This turns circle membership (“these points lie on the circle of known radius”) into simple distance-to-center equations you can subtract and solve systematically. It reduces reliance on fragile diagram interpretations.
items.reasoning: All correct attempts converged on the same reliable core method: place coordinates using the right angle at the vertex to turn the given segments into axis-aligned points, then use the circle-radius conditions for the two known points on the circle. Solving the resulting two equations by subtraction yields a linear relation between the center’s coordinates, and substituting back gives (typically) two symmetric candidate centers; the only remaining step is selecting the geometrically consistent one. Incorrect attempts failed in two main ways: (1) making an unjustified geometric claim (e.g., treating a chord/diameter/right-angle implication as forcing a particular circle-distance relation), or (2) selecting the wrong symmetric configuration due to a misread of the diagram or a coordinate-axis swap, producing the larger instead of the smaller candidate distance.

items.title: Extract a Linear Relation by Subtracting Circle Equations
items.description: After writing two “point lies on circle” equations, subtract them to eliminate quadratic terms and get a linear constraint on the center.
items.content: Given two points on the same circle, each yields an equation of the form (center−point)^2=r^2. Subtracting cancels the shared quadratic pieces (like x²+y²), producing a linear relation between center coordinates. This makes the remaining substitution step much cleaner and less error-prone.

items.title: Use the Correct Complement Event and Count It Reliably
items.description: When a probability involves “at least one/two of a local property,” switch to the complement event that forbids the property entirely and count that complement.
items.content: Define the complement as a clean constraint (“no two are adjacent”) rather than partially enforcing the opposite condition. Then count the complement using a method that is known to be correct for the cyclic structure, and only convert back to the target probability at the end.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently used a clean complement strategy (“no adjacency”) and relied on a correct, well-scoped counting formula for choosing non-adjacent vertices on a cycle. They also performed arithmetic checks after counting (e.g., simplifying the probability fraction and then the final requested expression). The incorrect attempts typically introduced an ad-hoc “gap distribution” count for the circular case but mishandled how the circle/gaps correspond to distinct subsets, leading to undercounting (e.g., getting 210 instead of 1750). Another failure mode was overcomplicating the complement (“at most one adjacent pair”) and then incorrectly reasoning about seat exclusions, which made the adjacency constraints non-exhaustive or double-counted/under-counted configurations.

items.title: Convert Divisibility to a Carry/Floor Condition
items.description: When a binomial coefficient is involved, translate “prime divides” into a carry-count or floor-discrepancy criterion rather than brute force factorization.
items.content: Use Kummer’s theorem to restate the exponent of a prime in a binomial coefficient as the number of base‑p carries when adding the relevant parts. Alternatively, with Legendre’s formula, focus on when the floor terms fail to cancel (the exponent becomes positive). This yields a direct, checkable condition for each candidate prime.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently used a robust “prime divisibility test” (Kummer’s theorem via carries, or an equivalent Legendre/floor-discrepancy criterion) and then combined it with a systematic search strategy: test candidates from largest down while justifying why higher candidates cannot work. The main failure mode in the incorrect attempts was reasoning that many primes automatically divide (or that a cancellation/pattern applies too broadly) without verifying the carry/floor condition; this led to accepting an unrealistically large prime. Another failure was “handwavy” handling of ranges (e.g., claiming only the k=1 term matters without checking the structural discrepancy that determines whether the exponent is actually positive). Overall, success depended on converting the abstract divisibility question into an explicit checkable condition (carry occurs / floor discrepancy ≥1), then proving the search is complete by bounding when carries can happen.

items.title: Justify Candidate Elimination with a Tight Upper/Lower Bound
items.description: When searching for the largest prime factor, prove a cutoff where divisibility becomes impossible, so checking smaller primes is guaranteed complete.
items.content: After deriving a carry (or floor-discrepancy) condition, determine for which primes that condition can even be satisfied (e.g., where a carry from a least-significant digit is possible). This produces a sharp range restriction, turning “try primes downward” into a complete proof that no larger prime can work.

items.title: Enforce a Frequency-Pattern Invariant
items.description: Translate “exactly two identical digits” into an explicit digit-frequency pattern and treat it as an invariant throughout counting.
items.content: Before counting, rewrite the condition as: the multiset of digits has exactly one digit with frequency 2 and two other digits with frequency 1 each (no triples/quads). This prevents subtle mistakes where a case accidentally allows additional repeats or miscounts the number of occurrences of the leading digit.
items.reasoning: The correct attempts consistently used a clean “structure of the digit multiset” model: with a fixed leading digit, they split into mutually exclusive cases based on which digit is the repeated one, then counted choices and placements in a way that exactly matches the “exactly two identical digits” constraint. The incorrect attempts mainly failed due to either (a) double-counting or including the wrong multiplicity pattern (e.g., treating the “repeated digit is 1” case as if it implied only one additional leading 1 when the constraint interpretation was inconsistent), (b) mixing up “exactly two identical digits” with “exactly one pair” plus allowing extra repeats, or (c) incorrect permutation counting (counting all permutations of a multiset then trying to “fix” the first digit afterward without enforcing the leading constraint consistently). Overall, success correlated with enforcing the constraint as an invariant (the digit-frequency pattern must be exactly one pair and two singles) and verifying case exclusivity and placement counts.

items.title: Validate the Geometry Before Using a Volume Template
items.description: Don’t apply a standard prism/pyramid/frustum volume formula unless you’ve confirmed the solid actually has the needed planar faces and linear “tapering” behavior.
items.content: When the volume method is unclear, first verify how the solid is formed (which vertices are connected, which edges are parallel, and what cross-sections are). Generic “base area × height × fraction” rules only hold for specific shapes (true prisms, tetrahedra, or prismatoids with known parallel faces), so justify or derive the decomposition rather than assuming.
items.reasoning: Many attempts tried to compute the height correctly from a single slanted edge length, but several failed by using an incorrect or unjustified volume formula for the 3D solid. The successful approach consistently (1) created a coordinate model that satisfied both the “parallel edge” condition and the “all other edges equal” condition, (2) used the edge-length constraint to get a definite perpendicular height, and then (3) decomposed/related the solid’s volume using a geometry argument that matches the actual 3D structure (often by splitting into congruent pieces whose volumes can be summed). The incorrect attempts either mixed up which base vertex connects to which top vertex (leading to inconsistent heights like negative h²), or they applied a generic prism/pyramid volume formula without verifying that the solid is actually a prism or that the chosen “pyramid” base corresponds to a real face/plane of the solid.

items.title: Encode Constraints with Coordinates (and Check Edge Adjacency)
items.description: Build a coordinate model that satisfies both length constraints and parallelism, ensuring the assumed point connections match the diagram’s adjacency.
items.content: Introduce coordinates for the base and top endpoints so the “upper edge parallel to base edge” condition is automatic. Then use a correct slanted-edge length equation to solve for the perpendicular height; if you get an inconsistency (e.g., impossible negative height squared), the coordinate pairing/adjacency is wrong, not the algebra.

items.title: Decompose Totals by Element Contributions
items.description: When summing a function over many subsets, rewrite the global sum as a weighted sum of each element’s net coefficient across all subsets.
items.content: For each element, compute how often it appears with a + sign versus a − sign; then multiply by the element and sum over elements. This avoids tracking full subset structure and turns the problem into sign-counting plus combinatorics.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent meta-strategy: rewrite the total as a sum of contributions from each element, where the sign of an element depends only on how many larger elements are also present. They then count the sign imbalance using a binomial alternating-sum identity, making cancellations systematic. The unsuccessful attempt’s failure is conceptual: it miscomputed the multiplicative factor for the surviving element by not correctly accounting for the number of choices from smaller elements (i.e., dropping a factor like the number of subsets containing that element). Overall, success comes from (1) choosing the right decomposition (element-wise contributions) and (2) correctly counting the independent choices that affect sign vs magnitude.

items.title: Translate Equal-Length Constraints into the Correct Invariant
items.description: When a problem gives an equal-length condition along a secant/chord, convert it into a corresponding power/radical-axis or circle-intersection invariant rather than directly assuming it fixes an unrelated geometric quantity.
items.content: Equal segments on a line through circle intersection points should be expressed via power of a point, radical axis, or a rigorously defined auxiliary circle whose intersections produce those segments. This prevents a common mistake: using the wrong chord (or wrong endpoints) and then computing a value that only matches a different “equalities” notion (like perpendicular distance to a radical axis).
items.reasoning: Successful attempts either leveraged the figure’s implied auxiliary circle/power-of-point/radical-axis structure to correctly connect the equal-chord condition to a fixed value (ending at the intended integer), and/or used coordinates but then recognized how to properly translate the geometric constraint (the equal segment lengths) into the correct algebraic relation. Unsuccessful attempts often stopped at computing the fixed “distance between centers to the radical axis” or treated certain chord intersections as if they were the relevant segments, leading to systematically wrong expressions (e.g., confusing which chord endpoints are being measured). Another failure mode was an unjustified inference that the equal-length condition forces the line to be a specific direction (like being perpendicular to the line of centers), without verifying that this indeed produces equal *physical* segment lengths for the chosen intersection points. Finally, several incorrect attempts relied on the auxiliary construction without proving the claimed radius relation, causing logical gaps even if the numeric coincidence looked plausible.

items.title: Reindex Subsequence Sums Explicitly
items.description: When asked for a sum over every other term (e.g., alternating indices), rewrite it as a new indexed series that matches the subsequence’s pattern.
items.content: After you express the general term, identify the subsequence (such as terms with indices following an arithmetic pattern) and parameterize it with a fresh index. This prevents mistakes caused by using the full series’ summation bounds/offsets for a reduced set of terms.
items.reasoning: The correct solutions use a consistent, index-driven representation of an arithmetic progression and then carefully re-express the target “every other term” sum by introducing a new index for the subsequence. The incorrect solutions mostly fail in one of two ways: (1) they make an arithmetic slip when transforming expressions for the subsequence or combining sums, and (2) they sometimes treat the subsequence sum as if it had already been computed from the full-sum without recalculating the corresponding offset/step contribution. items.title: Convert area-balance to signed-distance balance
items.description: When a line must split equal shapes into equal total area, rewrite the condition using signed perpendicular distances to the line.
items.content: For families of congruent regions (e.g., equal-radius circles) cut by the same line direction, the “area on one side minus the other” can be expressed as a common constant times the signed distance from the region’s center to the line. Then the global equal-area condition becomes a simple linear equation in the signed distances (often a sum of signed distances equals zero), avoiding messy segment-area formulas.

The successful attempts consistently used a robust “signed balance” principle: for equal shapes cut by a line, the difference between area on the two sides depends linearly (up to a common factor) on the signed perpendicular distance from each center to the line, so equality of total areas reduces to an algebraic condition on signed distances. They then translated that into a condition on the line’s normal vector / coefficients without needing the detailed circular-segment area formula.

The unsuccessful attempt that produced an extra candidate made an incorrect reduction: it replaced the required signed-area equality with an absolute-distance equality (and then tried to “discard” solutions via a heuristic about whether circles are fully on one side). The failure highlights the need to set up the balance using signed contributions and linear/odd dependence carefully, and to avoid introducing absolute values or geometric “validity” checks that lack a proof.

Finally, some incorrect attempts mixed in centroid arguments incorrectly (treating equal-area bisectors through a point as necessarily related to the centroid of the region). The correct solutions avoided that pitfall by using the dependence on signed distance and sum-of-signed-distances condition.

items.title: Refuse Global Closed-Form Guesses; Derive Locally First
items.description: When a recursive definition changes at a threshold, a single uniform formula across the whole domain is rarely consistent.
items.content: Start by computing several values near the threshold using the exact recursion and base rule. Let those evaluations reveal what invariant the function actually depends on (e.g., parity or residue), then generalize only after checking multiple adjacent instances. This prevents accepting an elegant but incompatible global guess.
items.reasoning: Successful work steadily avoided assuming a global closed form for the whole domain, instead computing values near the boundary and then using those computed values to justify a structured pattern (here, dependence on a simple invariant like parity/residue) below the threshold. It also maintained internal consistency by checking the inferred rule against multiple nearby cases before using it to evaluate the far target. By contrast, many incorrect attempts either (1) guessed an all-n form (e.g., constant shift or always-linear) without reconciling it with the recursion’s effect, (2) performed “crossing-threshold” reasoning about how many times the base rule applies without rigorously tracking the nesting of the function calls, or (3) invented plausible but unproven formulas (often involving floors/iterations) and then substituted without verification. The biggest failure mode was treating the recursion like a simple “add until ≥ threshold then subtract” process, ignoring that the function is nested and the outer application depends on the inner output.

items.title: Validate Any Inferred Pattern Against Multiple “Near-Boundary” Cases
items.description: After proposing a rule (even if it seems to fit), test it on several neighboring inputs to ensure recursion consistency.
items.content: For recursive problems, a small counterexample near the boundary is often enough to disprove an incorrect pattern. Successful attempts explicitly cross-checked the inferred behavior on a handful of inputs around where the definition switches, rather than extrapolating far immediately.

The biggest failure mode was treating the recursion like a simple “add until ≥ threshold then subtract” process, ignoring that the function is nested and the outer application depends on the inner output.

items.title: Track Nesting Effects Explicitly (Don’t Treat Recursion as Simple Iteration)
items.description: In nested recurrences, “crossing the threshold” does not mean the function applies only once; outer applications depend on inner outputs.

items.title: Preserve Structure with a Root-Reflecting Substitution
items.description: When a polynomial involves powers like \(z^6\) and \(z^3\), substitute a power of \(z\) to turn it into a simpler polynomial in a new variable.
items.content: Use substitutions such as letting an auxiliary variable be a fixed power of the unknown when the polynomial naturally groups into that form. Then translate back by using the corresponding relation between arguments (e.g., multiplying the argument by the power) rather than trying to solve the original equation directly.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently reduce the original polynomial using a substitution that preserves the root structure (expressing everything in terms of a smaller “derived” variable), then convert the resulting algebraic roots into arguments via polar/exponential form. They also carefully solve the modular angle relationship by enumerating the few values that could land in the specified interval. The incorrect attempt misidentified the arguments of the quadratic’s roots (using the wrong angles on the unit circle), which propagated through the modular arithmetic and produced an incorrect final angle. This shows the main failure mode: incorrect conversion from algebraic form to polar/argument form, especially when working with complex numbers on the unit circle.

items.title: Convert Complex Roots to Arguments via Correct Polar Form
items.description: Always compute the argument of complex roots carefully from their exact form (real/imag parts or known unit-circle locations), since a small angle mistake will invalidate later modular steps.
items.content: After finding algebraic roots like \(\frac{-1\pm i\sqrt{3}}{2}\), rewrite them in polar/exponential form or match them to standard unit-circle points to get the correct argument(s). This prevents propagation of a sign/angle error into the final “argument in an interval” calculation.

items.description: When you have an argument relation of the form \(m\theta \equiv \alpha \pmod{360^\circ}\), generate the full family of solutions and then test which ones fall in the required interval.
items.content: Use modular arithmetic to write \(\theta\) as a small arithmetic progression (from varying the integer multiplier), then check each candidate against the given angular bounds. This is more reliable than assuming only one case works, especially when multiple roots correspond to different \(\alpha\) values.

items.title: Extract Perpendicular Distances from Face Areas
items.description: When two given face areas share an edge, convert each area into the corresponding in-face altitude to that edge using the triangle area formula.
items.content: Use the common edge as a base in each face to solve for the perpendicular distances from the opposite vertices to that edge (within each face’s plane). This creates geometric “length parameters” that can be meaningfully combined across the dihedral angle.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently (i) computed the two “in-plane” perpendicular distances to the common edge from the face areas, then (ii) combined those distances using the dihedral angle via a correct sine factor, and (iii) converted the resulting product into volume with a correct global scalar factor (often through a triple-product or a carefully justified wedge/parallelepiped relation). Failures came from mixing incompatible “volume decomposition” formulas: using an incorrect prefactor or incorrectly treating how the edge length and perpendicular distances scale in the volume expression, or inserting the sine factor but with the wrong geometric interpretation/model (leading to systematic over/under-counting). Attempts that looked algebraically similar still failed because they effectively double-counted or misapplied the relation between the dihedral angle and the component used in the triple product / cross-sectional area.

items.title: Combine Across a Dihedral Angle Using a Verified Sine Factor
items.description: If you relate two perpendicular directions separated by a dihedral angle, the contribution in volume (or cross-sectional area) should scale with sin(dihedral).
items.content: Identify which vectors/distances are effectively separated by the given dihedral angle and ensure the sine factor corresponds to the angle between the directions you are “projecting into” the common perpendicular cross-sectional model. This prevents the common error of applying sin(θ) to the wrong angle or component.

items.title: Encode Feasibility as an Integer Set
items.description: Turn the original scoring/constraint system into explicit inequalities that define all possible integer values of the inferred quantity for a fixed observed score.
items.content: When the problem asks whether someone can uniquely recover an integer (like a count), first express the constraints so that for each candidate score you get a range (or discrete set) of feasible integers for that count. This prevents “almost right” solutions that satisfy simplified conditions but violate the original bounds (e.g., nonnegativity or total-attempt limits).
items.reasoning:
Successful attempts share a consistent core strategy: model the score in terms of underlying integer decision variables (correct/wrong/unanswered), then for a fixed score derive a *feasible set* of the quantity being inferred (number correct). They treat “John can determine uniquely” as an explicit *cardinality condition* on that feasible set (interval contains exactly one integer), and use the “any lower score” clause to interpret this as a *threshold/minimality condition* (uniqueness holds at the first score where the count collapses to one).
Unsuccessful attempts fail in one of two ways: (1) they mis-handle the interval/uniqueness logic (e.g., using incorrect equalities for floor/ceiling alignment, or assuming interval endpoints force uniqueness when rounding effects don’t), and/or (2) they misinterpret the “any lower” condition as “next-lower score must be non-unique” or as “smallest/ largest unique score” incorrectly, leading to contradictions. Some also introduce a correct framework but then compute using the wrong feasibility bounds (e.g., swapping parameters or using an incorrect upper bound), which shifts the uniqueness threshold.
Overall, the robust pattern is: be disciplined about translating all constraints into a single feasible-integer model, then verify uniqueness via exact counting (not heuristic “interval length” approximations), and finally apply the threshold condition by checking minimality against the derived model rather than ad hoc searching.

items.title: Uniqueness = “Exactly One Integer” (Don’t Guess with Length)
items.description: Establish uniqueness by proving the feasible set has cardinality one, using floor/ceiling arithmetic or equivalent exact counting—not by intuition about interval size.
items.content: Heuristics like “the bounds are close so it’s likely one” often fail because of discretization (ceilings/floors). Instead compute the feasible integer interval precisely and require that the number of integers equals 1 (i.e., lower bound’s ceiling equals upper bound’s floor, or equivalently count = 1).

Make that explicit: determine whether you need the smallest score > bound with uniqueness (or largest, depending on wording), and ensure the derived model matches that monotonic/threshold behavior.

items.title: Use a Gap-Based Adjacency Encoding

items.title: Don’t Stop at Periodicity—Track the Whole Orbit
items.description: When constraints imply a translation or periodicity, you still must determine which residue classes (or starting positions) are forced by the original root under all symmetries.
items.content: Composing symmetries often gives a periodicity relation like “if f(a)=0 then f(a+T)=0,” but that only propagates the roots you already know. To get the full forced root set, explicitly apply each symmetry (and their compositions) to the given root to find all distinct starting points/residue classes, then expand by the translation.
items.reasoning: The successful solutions consistently recognized that two reflection symmetries about distinct points generate not only periodicity, but also a multi-class “root lattice” (at least two residue classes). They then verified the full set of forced roots by tracking the orbit of the given root under both reflections and their composition, and finally counted roots in the interval by separating disjoint congruence classes. The incorrect attempts often stopped at deriving translation/periodicity from composing reflections, then wrongly assumed the given root class (e.g., one congruence class) is the entire forced set—missing that an additional reflection can shift the root to another residue class that periodicity alone does not determine.

items.title: Separate Forced Roots by Disjoint Invariants (e.g., Congruence Classes)
items.description: If forced roots arise from symmetries, classify them by an invariant such as position modulo the translation length, and count each class separately after proving disjointness.
items.content: After deriving a translation step from symmetry composition, different initial roots can land in different congruence classes modulo that translation. Counting is reliable only when you justify that these classes do not overlap (e.g., different residues mod the period), avoiding double-counting and missed roots.

Treating the reflections as generators, then repeatedly applying them to the initial root, reveals the complete orbit that must be zeros, rather than relying on one derived relation alone.

items.title: Prove “Largest Counterexample” via Coverage Beyond a Threshold
items.description: When asked for the largest integer that fails a representation property, you must both find failures and prove that all larger numbers succeed.
items.content: Use a two-part strategy: (i) explicitly test (or logically rule out) candidate representations for a finite range until you locate the maximal failing value, then (ii) give a general argument that any larger number has at least one valid representation. This prevents ending early based on empirical checking or unproven “eventual success” intuition.
items.reasoning: The successful approaches converged by (1) reducing the problem to checking which even totals can be formed from a restricted set (odd composites starting at the smallest value), (2) performing careful case checks near the suspected “last failure,” and crucially (3) supplying a justification that beyond some point representations always exist, so the largest counterexample must lie among the checked small cases. The unsuccessful attempts typically failed because they made an unjustified “sufficiently large” claim without a rigorous coverage argument, or they over-abstracted with heuristics about primes becoming sparse, leaving gaps where larger counterexamples could exist. Another recurring failure mode was inconsistent bookkeeping: listing candidate decompositions but not systematically ensuring all allowed summands were considered (leading to false “works”/“fails”). One attempt also mis-identified the threshold by confusing “largest even < minimum sum” with the actual largest counterexample once larger numbers begin to work.

items.title: Use Minimal Allowed Elements to Bound the Search Space
items.description: Start from the smallest permitted summands to get hard lower bounds and immediately eliminate impossible targets.
items.content: Translate the constraints into explicit numeric bounds (e.g., “smallest odd composite starts at …”), which yields a guaranteed range where representations are impossible. This both speeds up detection of initial failures and clarifies where the “largest failure” must be located.

items.description: After proposing a general construction (like fixing one summand), always verify it against the exact required property of the remaining summand.

items.title: Exploit Local Identities for Telescoping
items.description: Derive and use an identity involving neighboring terms to make the target product collapse.
items.content: When a sequence is defined recursively, multiply related terms (often adjacent ones) to reveal a simple constant or telescoping structure. This prevents relying on vague “full cancellation” reasoning and yields a compact expression for the whole product.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently exploited an invariant/telescoping relation derived directly from the recurrence, yielding a clean product without relying on error-prone “everything cancels” claims. Incorrect attempts typically overgeneralized cancellation, introduced unjustified shortcuts (e.g., treating a nested telescoping incorrectly, or concluding a factorial/square-root form), or dropped necessary remaining factors, leading to wrong final values. The key differentiator was grounding cancellation in a verifiable identity (like multiplying specific neighboring terms) and then computing the remaining small product carefully.

items.title: Verify Cancellation by Pairing, Not by Intuition
items.description: Check cancellation through explicit pairing that is justified by the recurrence rather than assumed.
items.content: Compute the product in a way that matches the recurrence structure (e.g., grouping terms so each factor’s appearance as numerator/denominator is accounted for). This avoids common mistakes where some factors cancel “in spirit” but actually leave leftover terms.

items.title: Do a Final Consistency Check Against a Derived Invariant
items.description: After transforming the product, confirm the result with a second viewpoint or the original recurrence.

This catches errors like missing endpoints or incorrect squaring/square-root manipulations.

items.title: Enforce Reality via Imaginary-Part Zeroing
items.description: When an expression is claimed to equal a real integer, set the imaginary part to zero to obtain necessary constraints on variables.
items.content: After expanding a complex expression, compute its real and imaginary parts separately. If the result is specified to be a (real) integer, the imaginary part must vanish, turning the problem into an integer equation that is usually much easier to solve.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently used the correct cube expansion of a complex number, then enforced that the target quantity is a (positive) real integer by setting the imaginary part to zero and solving the resulting integer factor equation. Incorrect attempts show two recurring failure modes: (1) algebra/sign mistakes in the complex expansion (especially the sign of the term added to the imaginary part), and (2) using the wrong expression for the real part when computing the final integer, effectively missing that the real part is not simply a “plus” version of the imaginary coefficient. A key pattern is disciplined separation into real/imaginary parts and verifying both against the original expression before concluding.

items.title: Reduce Indices Using Proven Periodicity
items.description: When a linear recurrence yields periodic behavior, reduce large indices modulo the period to convert the problem to a small finite computation.
items.content: First prove (not assume) that the terms repeat with some period, or that a fixed block sum is constant over blocks of that length. Then translate the goal (often a partial sum) into “value depends only on the index mod period,” ensuring the indexing convention matches exactly. This prevents wasted work and avoids remainder-offset mistakes.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently identified a strong structural invariant: the recurrence makes the sequence (and thus its partial sums) periodic, letting the given large indices be reduced modulo the period. They then computed a small number of terms (via expressions in the initial conditions or via small partial-sum relations) to solve for the needed values. Unsuccessful attempts either (a) claimed periodicity/period-sum facts without fully validating how they translate to the partial sums being evaluated at specific offsets, or (b) introduced extra relations that accidentally “cancel” crucial unknowns, leaving the final partial sum underdetermined or inconsistent (e.g., assuming a remainder case incorrectly or deriving the needed block/offset sum wrongly). The failures show the importance of verifying the mapping from periodicity of terms to periodicity (or constant block sums) of partial sums, and of ensuring the remainder-class used matches the indexing of the sums.

items.title: Translate Periodicity of Terms into Periodicity of Partial Sums
items.description: Having periodic terms is not enough; explicitly verify how that implies the corresponding property for partial sums you are asked to compute.
items.content: Check whether the sum over one full period is zero/constant and whether this implies S(n+period)=S(n) or S(n+period)=S(n)+constant. Use a small algebraic justification with block sums rather than relying on intuition. This is the key step that many incorrect attempts skip or apply with the wrong offset.

items.title: Convert the objective into a discrete “feasibility count” problem
items.description: When each variable can only take a small set of nearby integer values, use the global constraint to fix how many times each option must occur, then minimize the resulting worst-case penalty.
items.content: First restrict each decision to the only integer options that could possibly be optimal given the magnitude of the numbers and the form of the error. Then rewrite the sum constraint in terms of a count of each option, reducing the problem to choosing which specific indices get each option under a fixed count. This prevents wandering through too many combinations and makes the minimax step tractable.
items.reasoning:
Most successful attempts share the same correct global strategy: (1) restrict candidate integers by using the size of the numbers (here forcing each choice into a small set), (2) use the sum constraint to convert the problem into a fixed “how many of each option” selection, (3) minimize the maximum error by choosing the items that create the smallest worst-case penalty under that fixed count, and (4) prove optimality by a contradiction using the inequality form “if M were smaller, then certain choices would be impossible,” grounded in the discrete feasibility thresholds.
The only incorrect attempt follows the same approach and even arrives at the correct final value; so the key “failure” signal here is not mathematical correctness but the subtle risk in these problems: mixing up the sum-constraint algebra (e.g., using the wrong expression for the sum in terms of the count) and/or using strict vs non-strict inequalities when proving “cannot do better.” The successful attempts consistently keep these steps aligned and check feasibility properly.

items.title: Minimize a maximum by choosing the smallest “cost” items for the constrained role
items.description: For a minimax objective under a fixed number of “special” assignments (e.g., choosing some variables to round down), select those special indices with the smallest associated worst-case costs.
items.content: Once the counts of each integer choice are fixed, compute the error contributions for each possible assignment type. The maximum error is then the larger of the worst error among each type, so to minimize that maximum you assign the constrained role (the rarer or more damaging option) to indices with the smallest errors of that type. This “choose smallest penalties” move generalizes to many discrete minimax selection problems.

items.title: Prove optimality with a threshold feasibility contradiction
items.description: To show the minimax value cannot be improved, assume a smaller M and prove that the sum constraint would require an impossible assignment because too few indices can satisfy the tighter error threshold.
items.content: Use the inequality |integer - real| ≤ M (or < M) to characterize exactly which indices are allowed to take a given integer choice when M is small. Then show the required number of such indices (forced by the sum constraint) exceeds what’s feasible under the tighter M. Pay attention to strict vs non-strict inequality at the boundary to avoid off-by-one threshold mistakes.

items.title: Angle-Positivity Controls Sine/Cosine Signs
items.description: Before using sine addition formulas with square roots, use given inequalities to justify which cosines are positive and which sign of the identity applies.
items.content: When you rewrite trigonometric expressions using radicals (cos = √(1−sin²)), you must verify the relevant angles lie in a range that fixes the sign of the cosine. If the problem gives constraints like an angle sum being less than a certain bound, use it to ensure all half-angles are in the correct quadrant; otherwise the addition identity may need a different sign, and squaring later can silently produce contradictions.
items.reasoning: All correct attempts share the same reliable core: translate chord lengths into exact sine relations for half of the central angles, then use a sine addition identity with a consistent choice of signs guaranteed by angle size conditions (acute/positive cosines). The incorrect attempt fails because it mishandles sign/geometry in the sine-addition step (trying to force the “sum” form without fully justifying the sign), which leads to an impossible value after squaring; it then “rescues” the situation with unjustified claims. Successful solutions also avoid ad hoc guessing for intermediate variables and instead solve the resulting radical equation in a controlled way, keeping track of which quantities are nonnegative before squaring.

items.title: Controlled Elimination Then Squaring With Domain Checks
items.description: Turn the trigonometric relation into an equation involving only one radical at a time, then square only after confirming both sides are nonnegative.
items.content: A reliable pattern is: substitute known sine values, apply the addition identity to get a “sum of radicals” equation, isolate one radical, and square. After each squaring step, confirm the derived quantities are consistent with domain constraints (e.g., radicals correspond to acute angles), which prevents accepting invalid solutions created purely by algebraic squaring.

items.description: Rewrite each floor expression so all dependence on the integer shift is separated from the dependence on the fractional part.
items.content: For sums of the form \(\sum \lfloor a_i x\rfloor\), write \(x = m + f\) (or an equivalent scaling) with \(m\in\mathbb Z\) and \(f\in[0,1)\). Then use \(\lfloor a(m+f)\rfloor = a m + \lfloor a f\rfloor\) (when \(a\) is integer) to isolate a constant step coming from \(m\) and a bounded “residue generator” from \(f\). This prevents errors where one only studies a coarse fractional parameterization.
items.reasoning: The successful attempt reduced the floor-sum to a clean “integer part + fractional-part” decomposition, then exploited periodic arithmetic structure (a fixed step for the integer-part contribution). It further determined attainable residues by analyzing only where the fractional-part-dependent floor terms change, yielding a *small, exact* set of residues. The incorrect attempts commonly failed by (1) choosing an over-simplified parameterization (too coarse a partition of the fractional part), which caused missed/internal breakpoints; (2) incorrectly assuming values on a subinterval are captured by evaluating only endpoints; (3) doing residue counting without fully verifying that the set of residues is correct; or (4) confusing “distinct values vs attainable residues/classes,” leading to wrong density/overlap reasoning.

items.title: Find Attainable Residues by Tracking Only Jump Points
items.description: To determine which residues are achievable, analyze the fractional-part step function only at the points where any floor term changes.
items.content: When floor terms depend on \(f\) through \(\lfloor b f\rfloor\), their values change exactly when \(b f\) crosses an integer, i.e. at rational breakpoints. Enumerate the intervals induced by these breakpoints and compute the resulting constant residue contributions; the union over intervals gives the exact set of attainable residues. This approach avoids the common mistake of assuming floors are constant on overly large intervals or that endpoint sampling captures everything.

items.title: Validate “Endpoint Sampling” Assumptions
items.description: Never rely on evaluating a step function only at a few endpoints unless you can justify that nothing changes inside the interval.
items.content: For piecewise-constant expressions involving floors, the safe method is to partition using all breakpoints from every term, then evaluate within each resulting interval. If you skip some breakpoints (e.g., using a partition determined by one term but not the others), you can incorrectly conclude unattainable residues are impossible or attainable ones are missing. A quick verification pass against the full breakpoint set prevents these errors.

items.title: Use Reflection to Convert a Constrained Distance-Sum Minimum
items.description: When tangency implies an extremum of a sum of distances along a constraint line, reflect one focus across the constraint line so the minimum becomes a single straight-line distance.
items.content: Set up the ellipse as the locus where a constant equals a sum of distances to the two foci. If the tangency line is a straight constraint (e.g., the line where the extremum occurs), reflect one focus across that line; for points on the line, the reflected distance equals the original distance. Then the minimum of the sum becomes the shortest path, i.e., the direct distance between the other focus and the reflected focus.
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts converge on a robust geometric strategy: translate the tangency condition into an optimization of a sum of distances to the foci constrained to the tangent line. The key working idea is that for a lowest point where a horizontal line is tangent, the required “constant sum” value equals a minimum of a distance-sum expression, which can be solved cleanly via a reflection/shortest-path argument.

Unsuccessful attempts mostly fail by treating the tangency offset as if it always equals a semi-axis length in the fixed coordinate directions, ignoring ellipse rotation; this produces incorrect relationships between the geometric quantity “distance from center to the tangent line” and the semi-minor axis. Others introduce complicated projection/support-function formulas and then make coefficient/swap/arithmetic mistakes, which breaks the expected clean integer result. Some attempts also rely on heuristic “AIME integer expectations” instead of validating the derived geometry against the actual tangency constraints.

items.title: Validate Tangency Using an Optimization View, Not Axis-Aligned Assumptions
items.description: For a rotated ellipse, tangency to a coordinate line does not mean the center-to-line distance equals a semi-axis length unless the axes are aligned with that coordinate direction.
items.content: If the ellipse is rotated, “vertical distance from the center to the tangent line” generally mixes the ellipse’s principal directions, so equating it directly to the semi-minor (or semi-major) axis causes systematic errors. Instead, reduce tangency to a true extremum condition: the tangency point is where the constrained expression (often the focus-distance sum or a support function) attains its minimum/maximum.

items.title: Rotate Carefully: Prefer Reversible Coordinate/Vector Constructions
items.description: When using rotated axes/projections, compute the geometry from first principles and keep consistent definitions of which projection corresponds to which semi-axis.
items.content: Several failed attempts stem from swapping roles of coefficients or mixing which direction corresponds to the semi-axis in the support/minimum formula. A reliable pattern is: define principal-axis coordinates explicitly, express the tangent constraint as a line in those coordinates, and then apply a clear tangency condition (e.g., discriminant zero). This prevents silent sign/coefficient mistakes that destroy the final result.

items.title: Exploit Symmetry to Reduce State Space
items.description: When a stochastic process is symmetric over multiple equivalent states, track only the probability mass of equivalence classes instead of each state.
items.content: In a regular-graph random walk, many vertices are indistinguishable by symmetry relative to the starting position. Group them into “starting vertex” vs “any other vertex” and derive recurrences for these aggregated probabilities. This prevents errors that come from maintaining unnecessary detail or double-counting equivalent transitions.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently use a symmetry-reduced Markov chain: track only the probability of being at the starting vertex versus being at any other vertex, then derive a correct one-step recurrence. The main potential failure mode in unsuccessful drafts would be incorrectly handling transition probabilities (e.g., forgetting there is no “stay put” move on a tetrahedron walk, or miscounting how many outgoing choices lead to a particular target state) or mixing up per-vertex probability with total probability across multiple equivalent vertices. The correct solutions avoid this by explicitly relating “being at A” at time k+1 to “being not at A” at time k, or by verifying the reduction using total probability and symmetry.

items.title: Derive Recurrences from Exact Transition Logic
items.description: Build the update equation by explicitly counting which prior states can move into the target state in one step.
items.content: For each time step, express “probability of being in the target set next” as a sum over the prior states that transition into it, using the correct per-edge transition probabilities. A common pitfall is assuming a self-loop exists or using an incorrect factor when converting between per-vertex and total (across multiple vertices) probabilities. The successful solutions anchor the recurrence on the fact that from any vertex the walk moves to one of the other vertices with equal probability.

items.title: Convert Between Per-State and Total Probability Carefully
items.description: When using symmetry, ensure consistent conversion between a single-vertex probability and the combined probability across several equivalent vertices.
items.content: If three vertices share the same probability, the total “not in A” mass equals three times that per-vertex probability. Successful attempts either use total probability directly (e.g., 1 − p) or maintain both variables with the identity “A + 3·(other fixed vertex)=1.” This guards against subtle scaling mistakes that lead to incorrect recurrences even when the overall approach is right.

items.title: Count Player-Point Sums with Care (Avoid Double-Counting Games)
items.description: When summing contributions over players, re-derive from first principles how each game’s points are included—games can be counted 0/1/2 times depending on the summand definition.
items.content: Successful approaches explicitly define the summed quantity (e.g., “points earned by players in a subset against that subset”) and then count it by cases (within-subset games vs cross games). Unsuccessful attempts often incorrectly equate a player-sum to a game-sum, or assume each cross game contributes a fixed amount when in fact only one endpoint’s score is being counted.
items.reasoning: The successful solutions share a common successful pattern: (1) translate the condition into a “half of each player’s total points comes from a fixed subset” statement, (2) sum carefully in a way that counts player-point totals (not game totals) and uses the special “each of the ten lowest” clause to determine the subset’s internal contribution, and (3) use global totals (total points equals number of games) plus a second constraint for the complement group. Many failures come from miscounting how often a given game’s point contribution is included when summing over players—especially confusing “total points per game” with “sum of one endpoint’s points” across players—leading to incorrect linear equations or non-integer discriminants. Other failures attempt to “solve” for n using only global averaging without enforcing the internal consistency implied by the ten lowest players’ special case.

items.title: Exploit the “Subset-Self-Reference” in Extremal Constraints
items.description: If the condition refers to “the lowest scoring players,” use the special-case clause for players inside that subset to pin down that subset’s internal total.
items.content: Because the ten lowest players are guaranteed to be the same set when you apply the rule to them, you can determine an exact internal contribution (e.g., the total points produced by games inside the extremal subset). This converts an ambiguous global condition into a concrete equation before analyzing the complement.

items.title: Use Barycentric/Area Coordinates to Linearize Parallel-Slice Geometry
items.description: When parallel lines through an interior point create segments in different directions, model the point by barycentric/area proportions so each segment length becomes an affine-linear function.
items.content: Represent an interior point by its area ratios to the corner triangles; then lengths of segments cut by lines parallel to a side can often be expressed directly in terms of those ratios. This turns a geometric condition like “three segments are equal” into a small system of linear equations that is easier to solve and more reliable than guessing similarity roles.
items.reasoning: The successful attempt works because it uses a robust affine/area-proportion model (barycentric coordinates) and derives three linear relations by a correct “parallel slice” rule: the chord/segment length cut by a line parallel to a side is affine-linear in the corresponding area coordinate. The failures come from (1) asserting incorrect geometric mappings (centroid/incenter claims without proving equivalence), (2) using similarity statements that don’t match the actual “equal segments” definition (confusing side lengths of the inner triangle with the given chord lengths), and (3) relying on ad-hoc formulas for area/inradius that were not justified from the configuration, often leading to arithmetic or consistency issues.

items.title: Use “All Then Subtract Missing” for Permutation Sums
items.description: When summing several permutations of digits, compute the sum over all permutations first (which has uniform positional symmetry), then subtract any excluded permutations.
items.content: Summing all 3! permutations makes each digit appear the same number of times in each decimal place, yielding a clean linear form in the digits. If the problem asks for a subset (e.g., all but one permutation), subtract the omitted permutation’s numeric value to get the correct target sum; this prevents common counting errors that arise when counting directly within the subset.
items.reasoning: Successful approaches all (i) correctly modeled the sum N as a linear expression in the digits by carefully counting positional contributions across the listed permutations, most robustly by first summing all 6 permutations and then subtracting the missing one; (ii) then solved the resulting Diophantine equation using a combination of modular arithmetic (to restrict parity / congruence classes) and small bounded search (over digit ranges). Unsuccessful attempts commonly failed due to coefficient/counting mistakes (e.g., miscounting how many times each digit appears in each place among the selected permutations), or due to inconsistent algebra that led to impossible divisibility (signaling an earlier modeling error), followed by attempts to “patch” without re-validating the underlying coefficient model. Another failure pattern was using modular inverses or reductions with insufficient verification against original constraints, causing false dead-ends that should have prompted re-checking the setup.

items.title: Use Euler-Trail Modeling for Adjacent-Pair Counts
items.description: When you’re given counts of adjacent ordered pairs in a sequence, model the sequence as an Euler trail in a directed multigraph whose edge types correspond to the pairs.
items.content: Represent the sequence as a walk on “current symbol/state,” where each adjacent pair is a directed edge with a specified multiplicity. This turns the counting question into counting Euler trails using all edges exactly once, which is often easier to analyze with degree/invariant arguments and structured decompositions.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently (1) reformulate the string-counting problem as a walk/Euler-trail problem on a 2-vertex directed multigraph, (2) determine the forced start/end via indegree–outdegree balance, and crucially (3) count Euler trails by a structurally correct “block/run length distribution” or by a clean interleaving model that matches what choices are actually independent. The unsuccessful attempts either (a) overcount by treating “choose outgoing-edge types” as independent choices without enforcing the global coupling/consistency constraints of an Euler trail, or (b) invoke an advanced theorem (BEST) without correctly setting up the bijection/parameters (leading to a huge incorrect count), or (c) get the right forced endpoints but use a counting method that doesn’t reflect the correct number of degrees of freedom. The key distinction is not the model alone, but the correctness of the counting decomposition: successful solutions chose a state representation where the remaining freedom really is just distributing loop/self-edges among fixed block visits.

items.title: Force Endpoints via In/Out Degree Balance Before Counting
items.description: Determine the start and end symbols of the sequence by checking indegree–outdegree imbalances in the multigraph.
items.content: In an Euler trail, all vertices are balanced except possibly the start (outdegree − indegree = +1) and end (outdegree − indegree = −1). Computing this early prevents counting non-realizable trails and frequently collapses the problem to one fixed start/end case.

items.description: Convert “n equals the product of a specified subset of its divisors” into an equation involving τ(n) using the universal product-of-divisors identity.

Overall, correctness depends on using a reliable general invariant/identity to derive necessary and sufficient conditions, then verifying candidates and ensuring the enumeration is consistent with the full classification.

items.content: For problems with multiple absolute values, sign changes occur only at specific x locations determined by each absolute term. Setting intervals based on those breakpoints prevents missing parts of the region and avoids incorrect support like integrating over the wrong range.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts follow a consistent blueprint: (1) isolate the nonnegative quantity created by absolute values (often the “vertical half-height”), (2) determine the exact support in the slicing variable by solving when that quantity is ≥ 0 using a case split aligned with the absolute-value breakpoints, and then (3) integrate cross-sectional lengths using symmetry in y from |y|. The major failure in the incorrect attempt was not completing the support identification: it incorrectly concluded the region exists only on one interval, effectively treating points where the derived expression is negative as if they were irrelevant rather than understanding that they eliminate interior area (not just the boundary). Another subtle failure pattern is computational correctness: successful drafts keep the same piecewise structure throughout, while unsuccessful ones sometimes drop a needed interval or mis-handle when equality occurs.

items.title: Exploit Symmetry to Simplify Area Integration
items.description: If the equation depends on |y|, integrate only the top half and double (or integrate full height via 2|y|).
items.content: Symmetry reduces computation and error risk: you can focus on y ≥ 0 where |y| = y, then reflect. This also makes it easier to correctly define the cross-section length as 2f(x) (rather than re-deriving both sides).

items.title: Model “unique integer” as exact integer count in an open interval
items.description: Convert the uniqueness requirement into “exactly one integer lies in a specific open interval,” then count using floors/ceilings or neighbor checks.
items.content: When uniqueness is stated for an integer parameter constrained by inequalities, rewrite the constraints to isolate an expression that varies monotonically and yields an open interval (A,B). Then compute the number of integers in that interval via floor/ceiling formulas (or by showing the smallest feasible integer equals the largest feasible integer). This prevents mistakes where you only ensure the interval length is small but ignore where it sits relative to integers.
items.reasoning: The successful solutions consistently (1) translated the “unique integer k” condition into a clean integer-counting problem over an interval, and (2) carefully handled strict inequalities by using open intervals and floor/ceiling logic or an equivalent “neighbor integer” test. Failures largely came from incorrect uniqueness criteria (e.g., using length alone without validating endpoint strictness and whether one/two integers truly fit), from switching between k-based and n+k-based variables without fully re-deriving the corresponding bounds, or from asserting impossibility/monotonicity (“larger n must fail”) without a rigorous integer-count argument. Some attempts also did partial width reasoning but didn’t verify candidates near the threshold against the original strict inequalities.

items.title: Validate strict inequality endpoints after transformations
items.description: Any time you invert, cross-multiply, or introduce a new variable, re-check that strict bounds remain strict and that endpoints cannot become included accidentally.
items.content: Strict inequalities are especially error-prone when using floor/ceiling or when an endpoint might become an integer. A reliable workflow is: do algebraic transformations with sign/positivity checks, translate back into an open interval, and then verify a boundary or candidate integer really satisfies the original strict conditions. This catches common failures where an argument claims uniqueness but actually counts an endpoint incorrectly.

items.title: Convert Equal Angle Constraints into Fixed Included Angles
items.description: When multiple equal angles at the same vertex partition a full turn, determine their exact measure first.
items.content: If several rays around one point create equal angles and you know they fill 360°, compute each angle explicitly. This turns an ambiguous “equal angles” condition into a concrete included angle usable in length formulas, preventing later steps from being based on an incorrect angle value.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts share a clean, invariant-based structure: they correctly convert “three equal angles at the same vertex” into the corresponding 120° configuration, then use a universal quantitative bridge (Law of Cosines + Pythagorean theorem) to set up a solvable equation for the unknown length. Failures stem from overreaching with an unjustified geometric transformation/rotation inference (claiming a point must map to another preserving distances without establishing the needed correspondence), or from muddled/incorrect angle reasoning. In short: the correct solutions formalize the angle constraint rigorously and then translate to side-length relations; incorrect ones guess a consequence of symmetry/rotation without proving the mapping aligns with all constraints.

items.title: Use Law of Cosines as the Side-Length Bridge for Included Angles
items.description: After locking in included angle measures, express unknown lengths via Law of Cosines to get algebraic equations.
items.content: For triangles sharing a common included angle and with known adjacent sides to the vertex, the Law of Cosines directly produces quadratic expressions in the unknown side. This provides a reliable algebraic pathway that avoids relying on fragile geometric “symmetry guesses.”

items.title: Model “counted steps” as relative motion to the moving structure
items.description: When people “count steps” on a moving escalator, encode the count as motion relative to the escalator’s step-indexes, not as ground distance or pure walking distance.
items.content: Introduce a parameter for escalator step motion and write an equation where “steps counted” equals (person’s walking speed relative to the escalator) times (time). This prevents the common mistake of using the wrong frame (ground vs. escalator) and avoids contradictions where “counts” accidentally force the escalator length to equal the count.
items.reasoning: The successful attempt (1) stayed disciplined about what “counting steps” means: the count corresponds to motion relative to the escalator’s step-indexes, not to ground distance and not to a person’s own walking distance in a vacuum. It also used consistent direction/sign choices for the two walkers and expressed each observation as an equation relating time and relative speeds. Most incorrect attempts failed by conflating (a) “steps counted” with (b) “distance traveled relative to the ground” or with (c) “total escalator length,” leading to contradictions, negative speeds, or implausible results. Several wrong tries also drifted into assuming shared times without justification or used inconsistent relative-velocity expressions (e.g., adding where subtracting was required), which produced negative or self-contradictory parameters.

items.title: Use a consistent reference frame and sign convention
items.description: Pick a direction and a reference (e.g., bottom-to-top step index) and apply subtract/add relative speeds consistently across all walkers.
items.content: In escalator problems, walking up/down changes whether the escalator motion helps or hinders relative step-index progress; write each walker’s net relative speed with the correct sign. Inconsistencies often show up as negative speeds or impossible equalities—use that as a diagnostic to re-check direction logic.

items.title: Derive time separately for each observation, then eliminate variables
items.description: Turn each counting statement into its own time equation, then eliminate time using the shared escalator length.
items.content: For each person, express time as (escalator length) divided by their net relative-to-step-index speed. After that, “count = (walking relative speed)·time” gives a solvable system without assuming equal times or mixing endpoint assumptions.

This catches frame/sign errors early instead of chasing algebra to an incorrect numeric result.

items.title: Translate to an Explicit Start-Value Formula
items.description: Model the condition with variables for the starting term and express the sum as an equation that isolates the start.
items.content: When a problem says “N is a sum of k consecutive positive integers,” introduce a starting term and rewrite the sum with a closed form. Solving for the starting term turns positivity into a concrete inequality and makes integrality/divisibility conditions checkable.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same reliable backbone: translate “sum of k consecutive positive integers” into an explicit equation for the starting value, derive necessary structural constraints (divisibility/integrality), and then impose the positivity constraint as an inequality that bounds k. They also validate that the final chosen k really yields an integer starting point. The incorrect attempts mostly fail by (i) over-trusting a “maximum divisor” heuristic without re-checking the positivity/integrality simultaneously, (ii) mixing up which variable is being bounded (e.g., assuming the maximum k occurs at smallest starting value), or (iii) treating the inequality bound as sufficient without confirming divisibility/parity conditions.

items.title: Enforce Both Integrality and Positivity Separately
items.description: Divisibility/parity conditions and positivity constraints are independent and both must hold for a valid representation.
items.content: It’s common for k to satisfy the divisibility needed for the start to be an integer, yet still fail because the start becomes non-positive. Always handle “k must divide something” and “the start term must be ≥ 1” as two separate filters, not as one combined shortcut.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same reliable backbone: translate “sum of k consecutive positive integers” into an explicit equation for the starting value, derive necessary structural constraints (divisibility/integrality), and then impose the positivity constraint as an inequality that bounds k. They also validate that the final chosen k really yields an integer starting point. The incorrect attempts mostly fail by (i) over-trusting a “maximum divisor” heuristic without re-checking the positivity/integrality simultaneously, (ii) mixing up which variable is being bounded (e.g., assuming the maximum k occurs at smallest starting value), or (iii) treating the inequality bound as sufficient without confirming divisibility/parity conditions.

items.title: Use Positivity to Get a Global Upper Bound on k
items.description: Convert “starting term is positive” into an inequality that bounds k and sharply limits the search space.
items.content: From the start-value formula, derive an inequality like “start ≥ 1” that becomes an upper bound on k (often quadratic). This turns an open-ended maximization over divisors into checking only candidates near the bound.

items.title: Validate the Candidate by Computing the Start
items.description: After selecting a maximal candidate k, explicitly compute the implied starting value to confirm it’s a positive integer.
items.content: Even when k comes from divisibility reasoning, parity/integrality subtleties can still break feasibility. A quick direct computation of the start value acts as a final correctness check and prevents near-miss reasoning.

items.title: Don’t Assume the Maximum k Occurs at the Smallest Start
items.description: Heuristics like “maximize k by minimizing the starting integer” can be wrong because k affects both average and term structure.
items.content: For consecutive sums, the starting term and k are linked nonlinearly; minimizing the start (e.g., forcing the first term to be 1) only covers a special case. Use it as a possible lower-bound scenario, but don’t treat it as guaranteeing the global maximum without proving it.

items.title: Use Structured Factorization to Expose Cancellation
items.description: For products of similar algebraic expressions, factor them in a way that creates aligned subfactors across numerator and denominator.
items.content: When a ratio contains many terms of the same form with systematically related inputs, search for a factorization that produces common “pieces” (often quadratics or linear factors after completing squares). Successful solutions explicitly rewrote each quartic into two quadratics whose evaluations at shifted inputs match across the two products, enabling direct cancellation.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all share the same core technique: factor each quartic into two quadratics with matching “shifted” forms, then use multiset cancellation across numerator/denominator by aligning those shifted values. They also carefully verify the exact factorization (especially the coefficients) and then compare the resulting factors as multisets, not just sets, to ensure the leftover factors are correct. The incorrect attempts fail because they either (1) use a wrong or inconsistent factorization identity, (2) claim cancellations based on an incorrect shift/telescoping relationship without validating it algebraically, (3) confuse a factor that should cancel but doesn’t (leaving wrong leftovers), or (4) introduce an unproven telescoping formula and then trust the conclusion without confirming prerequisites.

items.title: Validate Factorizations by Re-Expansion Before Proceeding
items.description: After proposing an identity, immediately confirm it by expanding back to the original expression to avoid cascading errors.
items.content: Incorrect attempts frequently used a factorization identity that was misapplied (wrong coefficients or wrong matching of constants), yet proceeded as if it were valid. A reliable workflow is: propose the factorization → expand the product of factors → verify it equals the starting polynomial exactly.

items.title: Anchor Then Generalize Cycle Rules
items.description: When an iteration enters a cycle, first confirm exactly which iterate the cycle starts at before stating a general formula.
items.content: Compute a few consecutive values to locate the first repeat and determine the cycle’s entry point (the “preperiod”). Only then translate the cycle into a rule like “depends on parity of the index,” ensuring the index offset is correct to avoid shifted conclusions.
items.reasoning: Most attempts correctly identified the key reduction: iterating a function that depends only on the digit-sum, then observing the orbit reaches a short cycle. The incorrect attempt failed due to an index-alignment error when converting the observed cycle (which starts at a certain iterate) into a general parity rule for all larger n. The successful attempts consistently either (i) listed enough initial terms to anchor where the cycle begins, and/or (ii) explicitly aligned “n even/odd” with the correct starting index.

items.title: Index Alignment Validation Pass
items.description: After deriving a parity/cycle-based expression, validate it by plugging in the known terms near the cycle start.
items.content: A common failure mode is off-by-one or off-by-k errors in mapping “n even/odd” to which cycle element occurs. Fix this by checking the derived rule against the already-computed values at the first few indices within the cycle.

items.title: Model Divisors via Prime-Power Exponents
items.description: Represent all divisors by their prime-exponent vectors to avoid missing valid divisors.
items.content: When a number factors into distinct primes, every divisor corresponds to choosing exponents for each prime within allowable ranges. This prevents the common mistake of treating divisors as if they were only powers of a composite base, which can exclude divisors where prime exponents differ.
items.reasoning: The correct solutions all explicitly used the prime-factor structure of the number, representing every divisor as a product of prime powers and then converting the “multiple of another power” condition into independent exponent inequalities. The incorrect solutions repeatedly assumed divisors of the form “powers of 10” (i.e., only varying a single exponent), which silently dropped the fact that divisors of a composite number with primes 2 and 5 need not contain equal amounts of 2 and 5. That mismatch between the representation of divisors and the actual divisor set is the core failure mode.

items.title: Translate “Multiple of” into Exponent Inequalities
items.description: Convert divisibility conditions into constraints on prime exponents.
items.content: If you need a divisor to be an integer multiple of another integer, rewrite both numbers in prime powers and enforce that each prime exponent in the divisor is at least the corresponding exponent in the target. This turns an algebraic divisibility statement into simple counting of allowable exponent ranges.
items.reasoning: The correct solutions all explicitly used the prime-factor structure of the number, representing every divisor as a product of prime powers and then converting the “multiple of another power” condition into independent exponent inequalities. The incorrect solutions repeatedly assumed divisors of the form “powers of 10” (i.e., only varying a single exponent), which silently dropped the fact that divisors of a composite number with primes 2 and 5 need not contain equal amounts of 2 and 5. That mismatch between the representation of divisors and the actual divisor set is the core failure mode.

items.title: Use Independent Counting Across Exponents
items.description: When constraints separate by primes, count choices per exponent and multiply.
items.content: After converting conditions into independent inequalities for each prime exponent, the count factors into a product of the number of valid choices for each exponent. This yields clean totals and reduces the risk of under/over-counting due to entangling conditions incorrectly.

A mismatch (like assuming divisors are only powers of 10 instead of all 2–5 exponent combinations) leads to systematic counting errors even if subsequent inequality logic is correct.

items.title: Use Vector Dot/Cross to Avoid Angle Misidentification
items.description: Compute \(\tan\) of an angle via cross/dot products instead of relying on which “opposite/adjacent” leg a diagram suggests.
items.content: When the angle’s right-triangle decomposition is ambiguous, represent the angle using two rays as vectors and use \(\tan\theta=\frac{\|\vec u\times \vec v\|}{\vec u\cdot \vec v}\) (with correct absolute/sign handling). This is harder to misapply than splitting angles into smaller right triangles and prevents systematic geometry mistakes.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a robust coordinate/vector method: place the altitude foot and the two base points so the altitude segments fix the x-coordinates, then compute the tangent of the angle at the vertex using a general formula involving dot and cross products. Unsuccessful attempts largely fail by mis-identifying which right triangle pieces correspond to the given angle, then forcing tangent addition or opposite/adjacent ratios with inconsistent geometry; some also get stuck in “AIME must be integer” interpretation without actually re-deriving the correct relation. The key differentiators are (1) using a representation where the given segment lengths directly determine coordinates, (2) computing tan(angle) in a coordinate-invariant way (cross/dot), and (3) validating the algebraic relation carefully (especially signs in the dot product/cross product formula) rather than retrofitting to an expected answer.
