<content>When the angle’s right-triangle decomposition is ambiguous, represent the angle using two rays as vectors and use \(\tan\theta=\frac{\|\vec u\times \vec v\|}{\vec u\cdot \vec v}\) (with correct absolute/sign handling). This is harder to misapply than splitting angles into smaller right triangles and prevents systematic geometry mistakes.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Fix a Coordinate System from Given Lengths</title>
<description>Place points so the given segment lengths become direct coordinate differences, then compute the unknown using geometry/algebra.</description>
<content>Choose coordinates where the altitude foot is at the origin and the base segment splits correspond exactly to x-coordinates; this turns length data into immediate coordinate constraints. It reduces the chance of mixing up which segment is adjacent/opposite because everything is derived from the same placement.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Validate Transformations Against the Original Definition</title>
<description>After any transformation (sign flips, tangent addition, slope formulas), re-check against the original definition of the target angle.</description>
<content>Many failed attempts stem from using a tangent identity with a wrongly ordered angle sum/difference or an inconsistent slope/leg pairing. A quick verification step—confirming whether the computed \(\tan\theta\) matches the intended angle between the two specified rays—catches these issues early.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Don’t Let “Expected Integer” Drive Geometry</title>
<description>A guessed AIME/AoPS-style integrality requirement should not replace correct derivation of the governing equation.</description>
<content>When a result is non-integer, treat it as evidence the angle relation was set up incorrectly, not as permission to reinterpret formulas ad hoc. Correct practice is to revisit the mapping from the given angle to the algebraic expression (dot/cross or correct tangent identity), then recompute.</content>
</memory>
</memories>

items.title: Derive a reliable transform from the recursion, then verify candidates
items.description: Before committing to a closed form, explicitly rewrite the functional equation into a usable transformation rule and then check the candidate against all original constraints.
items.content: Start by algebraically isolating the term that advances the arguments (e.g., expressing f(x, x+y) in terms of f(x,y)). When you guess a closed form, run a full verification: diagonal condition, symmetry condition, and the original (not simplified) recursion. This prevents “looks right” failures where only an intermediate or easier relation holds.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently (1) used the functional equation to derive a reliable transformation rule relating f(x,y) to f(x,x+y) (and via symmetry to also relate other directions), (2) then identified the right invariant/closed form by expressing the dependence on gcd, and (3) verified the proposed form against all three given conditions. Unsuccessful attempts typically either guessed plausible but unverified closed forms (failing diagonal/scaling checks), or proposed an incorrect “invariant” (e.g., gcd itself or max) without satisfying the full recursion, or performed Euclidean-algorithm reductions but with algebra/logic that became unreliable or incomplete. The key differentiator was disciplined transformation + invariant identification + full condition checking.

items.title: Expect Euclidean-algorithm behavior and seek gcd-based structure
items.description: For functional equations that shift arguments by addition and include symmetry, it’s often fruitful to model the solution as depending on gcd via factoring out common divisors.
items.content: When the relation repeatedly combines x and y through steps resembling the Euclidean process, try writing x = d·a and y = d·b with d = gcd(x,y). Then reduce the recursion using properties like gcd(a, a+b)=gcd(a,b). This typically converts a messy two-variable recursion into a checkable identity in a and b.

items.title: Validate “invariant” claims by testing against a counterexample pathway
items.description: If you think a quantity is invariant under a transformation step, confirm it by plugging into the functional equation rather than trusting the heuristic.

items.title: Model “last k digits” as a modular congruence
items.description: Convert the digit-ending requirement into an explicit congruence modulo the appropriate power of 10.
items.content: When a problem asks for the last k digits of an expression, rewrite it as a congruence modulo 10^k. This prevents ad-hoc guessing and makes the algebraic solution path (e.g., modular arithmetic) legitimate and complete.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently used a reliable modular-constraint pipeline: (1) translate “ends in” to a congruence mod 10^k, (2) factor the modulus into coprime parts, (3) solve the congruence on each part, and (4) combine via CRT with a final verification and a minimality argument. Failures came from (a) random or incomplete residue searching without proving that the found residue is correct, (b) arithmetic mistakes during modular reductions, (c) switching to direct brute-force without justifying minimality, and (d) claiming a “known” answer/residue without deriving or verifying it. Another pattern: when modular solving was started correctly, the correct solution was obtained by systematically determining the needed residue class modulo the prime-power factor (often with invertibility/exponent reasoning), then enforcing extra conditions (like parity from the mod 8 constraint) when combining.

items.title: Use CRT by factoring the modulus into coprime components
items.description: Solve the congruence separately modulo coprime factors, then combine solutions with CRT.
items.content: If the modulus factors into coprime pieces, split the problem into independent subproblems modulo each factor. After finding residue classes for each subproblem, CRT guarantees consistent recombination; verify the combined candidate against the original congruence to catch errors.

items.title: Model the “same shift” constraint using parameterized roots
items.description: Turn “adding the same integer makes three numbers into squares of consecutive arithmetic terms” into explicit equations with two parameters.
items.content: When a common modification is applied to multiple expressions, introduce variables for the underlying structured object (e.g., the arithmetic progression’s first term and common difference) and write each given value plus the shift as a square of the corresponding progression term. This prevents relying on guesswork about which square roots are involved.
items.reasoning:
Successful attempts converge on a robust modeling of the condition: after adding the same integer, the three results are squares of an arithmetic progression of bases (roots in arithmetic progression). They then use the ordering (since the added constant preserves inequalities) to justify matching the smallest-to-smallest, middle-to-middle, etc., and eliminate the unknown shift by subtracting equations. The key algebraic “signal” is that differences between squares in an arithmetic progression have a predictable form involving only two parameters, enabling elimination (e.g., taking a difference of the two gap equations to get a simple expression like a constant times the square of the common difference).

Unsuccessful attempts typically fail by assuming an incorrect structural interpretation (e.g., that the squares correspond to consecutive integers rather than consecutive terms of an arithmetic progression with unknown step), or by making inconsistent/too-strong ordering assumptions about which given number corresponds to which square root. Some also attempted to use only pairwise gaps without verifying that the gap equations correspond to adjacent terms in the required arithmetic progression of roots.

items.title: Exploit Shift-Polynomial Structure
items.description: When coefficients arise from shifting a polynomial sequence, treat the whole sum as a polynomial (in the shift) and extrapolate.
items.content: If a quantity is formed as a fixed linear functional applied to (j+m)^d, then as a function of the shift m it is a polynomial of degree ≤ d. Knowing the value at enough consecutive shifts determines the whole polynomial, so you can compute the next shift directly without solving for the individual variables.
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts all recognize the core structural pattern: the given expressions are the same linear functional applied to a “shifted” quadratic sequence, so the resulting value as a function of the shift is itself a quadratic (or has constant second differences). This lets you extrapolate from a small number of consecutive shifts to the desired one using either polynomial-in-shift reasoning or finite-difference reasoning, both of which rely only on general degree behavior (degree 2 ⇒ constant second difference).
The incorrect attempts fail mainly by mis-indexing the target shift (claiming the asked sum corresponds to the wrong S_m), or by performing linear system setup with incorrect relationships between the moments and the shift formula (e.g., using inconsistent definitions so the computed parameters don’t correspond to the actual given equations). One incorrect attempt also tries to overcomplicate using high-degree vanishing polynomials without correctly constraining which moments are actually known, leading to unjustified extraction of T0/T1.

items.title: Recognize When the Problem Is a Known Identity
items.description: When a problem presents a highly specific numeric structure tied to a famous conjecture or counterexample, treat it as a “recognition task” and use the known identity directly.
items.content: If the question matches a well-documented counterexample or construction, the fastest route is to recall the established equality and extract the requested parameter from it. This avoids spending time on ad hoc derivations that are unlikely to reconstruct a known identity from scratch.

items.title: Model Forbidden Differences as a Graph Independence Problem
items.description: Turn “no pair differs by X or Y” into an independent set problem on a graph whose edges represent forbidden differences.
items.content: When constraints are about pairwise differences, encode them as edges between integers (or as moves on residues). Then rephrase the goal as finding the maximum independent set size, which clarifies what must be avoided and suggests using periodic structure for tractability.
items.reasoning: The correct solutions succeed by using a clean, verifiable modular/graph perspective: represent forbidden differences as edges between integers, then pass to a residue-class graph where the structure becomes periodic and analyzable. They also carefully construct a global set using a repeating residue pattern and then justify optimality with a matching upper bound per modulus block. The failed attempts typically (1) pick an unproven or incorrect local bound (e.g., claiming “at most 2” or “at most 6” per fixed-length window without rigorous justification), (2) mix up residue-level independence with actual difference constraints at finite interval boundaries, or (3) propose constructions whose cross-block differences are not actually verified, leading to off-by-one errors in the final count. Attempt 2 in particular succeeds in both constructing an independent residue set and correctly accounting for incomplete final blocks.

Compute the maximum independent set size on the residue graph (e.g., via cycle size/alternating selection), then lift it to actual numbers by taking all integers in independent residue classes.

items.title: Use Order-Statistic Counting with a Monotone “≤ N” Function
items.description: Turn “find the kth term of a filtered increasing sequence” into “find the smallest N where the count of valid numbers ≤ N reaches k”.
items.content: When you have an increasing list defined by excluding objects (squares/cubes/etc.), define a function that counts how many objects remain valid among {1,…,N}. This function is monotone in N, so the kth term corresponds to the smallest N where the count is ≥ k (and often exactly k if you check minimality).
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently model the problem as an “order statistic via counting”: define a monotone function that counts how many valid (included) integers are ≤ N, then locate the unique N where this count hits the target, checking neighboring values to confirm minimality. The unsuccessful attempts often made the correct counting model but erred in the final “which integer is the 500th?” logic—confusing “count of valid numbers ≤ N equals 500” with whether N itself is valid, leading to off-by-one conclusions (e.g., concluding the 500th term is a forbidden square). Successful work also carefully verifies which floor thresholds change between consecutive candidates, ensuring the count really jumps at the correct N.

items.title: Validate Inclusion–Exclusion Overlaps with the Correct Exponent/Structure
items.description: When counting numbers excluded by multiple conditions, compute the intersection using the exact characterization of “both at once”.
items.content: For exclusions like “square or cube,” the overlap is not double-counted blindly; instead identify the precise common form (e.g., being simultaneously a square and a cube implies a specific power) and count those with the appropriate floor expression. This prevents systematic counting errors that look minor but shift the kth term.

items.title: Convert Ratio Conditions into Integer Divisibility
items.description: When an equation derived from angle/geometry gives a rational formula for one polygon’s sides, rewrite it so integrality becomes a clear “divides” condition.
items.content: After substituting standard expressions (e.g., interior angle of a regular n-gon), rearrange until the unknown integer appears in a fraction of the form r = (C·s)/(K−s). items.title: Track each percentage’s target population
items.description: Assign every given percentage to the specific group it applies to (survivors vs composition vs sampling fraction) before multiplying them together.
items.content: In population problems with multiple assumptions, it’s easy to incorrectly combine factors by applying a “loss” rate to the wrong base. Write down explicitly whether a percentage describes “remaining among May-1 fish,” “portion of September fish that came from May,” or “fraction tagged in the population,” and only then convert to tagged counts or totals.
items.reasoning: The successful solutions consistently model two separate “filters” from May to September: (1) survival of the May-1 stock (a multiplicative factor applied to totals and to tagged survivors), and (2) composition of the September population (a multiplicative factor describing what fraction originated in May). They then use representativeness to equate a tagged fraction in a sample to a tagged fraction in the total population, ensuring the tagged count being used is the one implied by the survival assumption. Failures typically come from mixing up which percentage applies to totals vs survivors (using 25% as “remaining” instead of “gone,” or applying it to the wrong group), or from not tracking whether the tagged count in September should be 0.75 times the initially tagged amount. Another failure pattern is inconsistent equation chaining: computing a September total from tagged proportions, but relating it back to May using the inverse of the intended percentage.

items.title: Use representativeness to form the correct tagged-fraction equation
items.description: When a sample is stated to be representative, set the sample’s tagged-to-total ratio equal to the population’s tagged-to-total ratio.
items.content: Representativeness means fractions match: if you observe x tagged in a sample of size y, then tagged/population = x/y. The key is to use the tagged count in the numerator that your other assumptions imply (e.g., after accounting for survival), rather than using the sample count directly as if it were the population tagged count.

items.title: Use Unit Vectors to Get Internal Angle Bisectors
items.description: For an internal angle bisector, compute it via the sum of the two unit direction vectors along the rays.
items.content: When you need the bisector direction analytically, convert each side direction vector into a unit vector, then add them; the resulting direction is guaranteed to bisect the angle internally. This avoids ambiguity about which bisector (internal vs external) you are computing and removes the need for sign-case decisions.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently used a robust bisector characterization: the internal bisector direction is the sum of the *unit* direction vectors along the two sides (or equivalently, the correct “±” choice in the distance-to-lines equation). The main failure modes were (1) choosing the wrong sign branch when using the distance-to-lines criterion (leading to the external bisector), and (2) mishandling the required target form “ax+2y+c=0” by scaling incorrectly, causing mismatched coefficients or non-integer outputs. Another failure came from an arithmetic/direction error (using a non-perpendicular normal or incorrect scaling), producing a direction that no longer matched the intended bisector.

items.title: Model Roots of Unity as Modular Exponent Arithmetic
items.description: Translate equations like \(x^n=1\) into angle/exponent congruences modulo 1 to turn multiplication into modular addition.
items.content: When sets are defined by exponent equations, rewrite elements as exponentials \(e^{2\pi i k/n}\) and convert products into sums of rational exponents. Then count distinct products by counting distinct residue classes of the resulting linear expression modulo the common denominator, reducing the problem to modular arithmetic or subgroup size.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a robust two-step strategy: (1) convert the algebraic conditions defining the sets into an explicit “exponent arithmetic” model (roots of unity correspond to rational angles modulo 1), and (2) count distinct outcomes by studying the reachable residues as an additive subgroup in a modular integer ring. A key success factor is validating that distinct residues correspond to distinct complex numbers (injectivity of the exponential map on those specific denominators). The incorrect attempt that yielded a smaller count made an unjustified claim that the set of reachable residues is exactly the multiples of a factor (it implicitly over-constrained the modular expression by mis-handling how coefficients vary modulo the modulus). Another incorrect attempt/line of reasoning showed the danger of relying on “order/lcm” statements without a complete justification of the converse (“every such root occurs”), even if the subset containment is true; attempts that only proved “all elements are 144th roots” but didn’t correctly establish “all 144th roots occur” risk wrong cardinalities.

items.title: Count via Generated Subgroups, Not Informal Divisibility
items.description: To find how many distinct linear-combination outcomes occur mod \(N\), treat the set as a subgroup generated by the coefficients and use \(\text{size}=N/\gcd(N,\text{generators})\).
items.content: If reachable residues have the form \(a\alpha+b\beta \pmod N\), identify the subgroup \(\langle \alpha,\beta\rangle\subseteq \mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z\) and compute its order using gcd. This avoids common mistakes where one factor is pulled out and incorrectly assumed to capture all residues (leading to undercounting).

items.title: Encode Leading-Digit Conditions as a Fractional-Part Inequality
items.description: Convert “the first digit is d” into a half-open interval for the fractional part of k·log10(x).
items.content: For numbers like a^k, write the condition “leading digit equals d” as an inequality between d·10^m and 10^(m+1), then take logs to reduce it to an inequality involving the fractional part of k·log10(a). This creates a clean counting problem over k with {kα} lying in a fixed interval, and avoids ad-hoc reasoning about digit patterns.
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts consistently: (1) translated “leading digit is 9” into a sharp inequality in terms of scientific notation and then into a condition on the fractional part of k·log10(9); (2) used the given digit-count/first-digit information to pin down the fractional part for a specific k (the endpoint), which resolves the otherwise ambiguous rounding that occurs when counting points landing in a half-open interval; and (3) performed an explicit floor/ceiling selection justified by that endpoint inclusion (rather than relying on a vague “uniform distribution” argument).

Finally encode the covering/outer constraint as the correct center-to-origin (or center-to-boundary) distance relation, and only then solve for the unknown radius.

items.title: Translate Fraction Conditions into Integer+GCD Constraints
items.description: Convert “denominator in lowest terms” and inequality on the fraction into an explicit range for the numerator plus a coprimality condition.
items.content: When a fraction is constrained to have a given denominator in lowest terms, rewrite it as a numerator over that denominator with a gcd constraint (numerator coprime to the denominator). Then turn any inequality on the fraction into a strict/weak inequality on the numerator’s integer range before doing any counting or summing. This prevents common errors where the inequality is accidentally applied to the wrong quantity.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent modeling approach: translate the rational constraint into an integer numerator constraint with a clear coprimality condition, then compute the integer sum using periodicity modulo the denominator. The incorrect attempts fail mainly by mishandling the numerator range (e.g., accidentally restricting to too small a set), miscounting the number of blocks/residues included in the interval, or forgetting that the “coprime residues” set must be summed with correct multiplicities across the whole allowed range. Several wrong solutions also treat “less than” as if it implied a smaller bound on the numerator than it actually does, or they reuse the reduced-residue sum without adding the shift-by-block (the 30k term) correctly.

items.title: Use Mod-Periodicity, Then Account for Partial Blocks Correctly
items.description: For sums of integers filtered by gcd conditions, exploit periodicity modulo the denominator but compute the contribution of both full and partial residue blocks.
items.content: If the property “coprime to M” depends only on the residue class modulo M, group numbers into blocks of length M and reuse the same reduced residue set in each full block. For the last incomplete block, recompute which residues actually occur (based on the endpoint) so you don’t overcount or undercount residues. This is the most frequent source of off-by-one block-count mistakes.

items.title: Use the “digit set → unique increasing string” Bijection
items.description: When order is forced (e.g., strictly increasing digits), count by choosing the underlying set, since each set corresponds to exactly one valid arrangement.
items.content: If a constraint like “digits strictly increase left-to-right” forces a unique order, then every valid object corresponds one-to-one with a choice of distinct digits (or other atomic components). This simplifies counting and reduces the risk of under/over-counting from unnecessary permutations or ordering logic.

The incorrect attempt failed by double-counting: it counted configurations where 0 is used and also separately counted configurations that already cover those cases in the “0 not used” count, effectively including overlapping scenarios or using an incorrect decomposition. The successful approaches also carefully checked the allowed length of the numbers based on the available distinct digits, but the main distinguishing factor is the correct, non-overlapping partition/counting model.

items.title: Model the decimal as a fixed-denominator fraction, then reduce via gcd
items.description: Convert a repeating decimal constraint into an exact rational form with a known denominator, then express the reduced numerator in terms of gcd with that denominator.
items.content: When a problem specifies a fixed repeating length, rewrite the value as an integer block over a constant denominator (e.g., “block / 999”). Then reduce symbolically: the reduced numerator is the original block divided by gcd(block, denominator). This creates a clean bridge from digit constraints to number-theory counting/existence questions.
items.reasoning:
Successful solutions (Attempt 1 and 2) both anchored on a reliable algebraic model of the repeating decimal as a fraction with a fixed denominator, then carefully analyzed which reduced numerators are actually attainable under the constraint that the original numerator range is bounded away from the endpoints. The key success came from translating “distinct reduced numerators” into a number-theory/existence question about whether, for a given candidate numerator, there exists some original numerator whose gcd reduction produces it—while respecting the upper bound.
Unsuccessful solutions commonly failed in one of two ways: (1) they incorrectly assumed a simple characterization of attainable reduced numerators (e.g., “all numerators coprime to the fixed denominator”), without proving existence under the bound; or (2) they counted via unions/sums over possible denominators without addressing overlap and/or without justifying why each counted numerator truly corresponds to some allowed original numerator. Another failure mode was using ambiguous “can choose N=m” arguments without re-checking that the resulting gcd reduction would preserve the intended numerator.

items.title: Prove attainability under bounds (don’t just characterize candidates)
items.description: When counting distinct reduced values from a bounded set of inputs, verify that each candidate reduced numerator is actually achievable within the original allowed range.
items.content: It’s not enough to list necessary conditions like “coprime to the denominator”; you must show there exists at least one original numerator in the permitted interval whose gcd-reduction yields that candidate. A common pitfall is to pick a convenient preimage (like choosing input equal to the candidate) and forget that the gcd-reduction may change it or violate the bounds.

items.title: Avoid naive counting over reduced denominators without overlap control
items.description: When counting distinct numerators that arise from multiple possible reduced denominators, ensure your counting method doesn’t double-count or assume a bijection you haven’t proven.

items.title: Use gcd uniqueness carefully: equality of reduced fractions vs equality of numerators
items.description: Distinguish between uniqueness of reduced fractions and non-uniqueness of numerators across different reduced fractions.
items.content: Reduced fractions are unique in lowest terms, but the same integer numerator can appear in multiple reduced fractions with different denominators. If a solution counts numerators by treating denominators as implicitly determined by the numerator, it must justify that assumption; otherwise it can under/overcount.

items.title: Model “No Carry” as Column-wise Digit Inequalities
items.description: Translate “no carrying” into explicit per-digit column constraints on the actual addition.
items.content: When a problem mentions “no carry,” do not rely on intuition about a single digit; rewrite the condition as: for every base-10 digit position, the sum of the corresponding digits in the two addends must be ≤ 9. This prevents logical gaps caused by carry propagation changing higher digits.
items.reasoning: The correct attempts succeeded because they identified the right structural condition for “no carry” in adding consecutive integers: the digits of the larger number and the effect of incrementing it by 1 (especially through trailing 9s) must be checked at every digit column of the actual sum, not just inferred from a single digit (like the units). They then counted systematically by parameterizing the run of trailing 9s and enforcing digit-sum inequalities, ensuring the mapping between “admissible numbers” and “admissible consecutive pairs” is complete. The incorrect attempts largely failed by using an oversimplified equivalence (e.g., “no carry iff the last digit isn’t 9”) or by assuming increment behavior (n→n+1) implies identical digits in the higher positions, without validating that carry propagation doesn’t change those digits; this leads to undercounting/overcounting. Another failure mode was mishandling boundary inclusion of the last element in the set and whether the consecutive pair starting at that boundary should be included.

items.title: Parameterize Increment Effects via Trailing Runs
items.description: For consecutive integers, analyze the digit changes of adding 1 by parameterizing the trailing pattern (e.g., trailing 9s).
items.content: In problems involving n and n+1, the difference between them is controlled by how far a carry would propagate when incrementing n. Classify cases by the length of the trailing block that changes (such as a run of trailing 9s), then enforce the digit-sum constraints only where the digits truly differ.

items.title: Translate face areas into true geometric lengths
items.description: When volume depends on heights, first extract the relevant perpendicular distances directly from area formulas to ensure they are lengths (with correct dimensions).
items.content: Use area = (1/2)·base·height in each face that shares an edge, so the “heights” you compute are perpendicular distances within those face planes. This avoids later mistakes where one might incorrectly combine quantities that are actually areas or non-perpendicular offsets.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently: (1) converted each given face area into a concrete “altitude to the common edge” (a true length within that face), then (2) used a correct geometric/coordinate argument to introduce the dihedral angle via a sine factor, and crucially (3) used an exact tetrahedron-vs-prism (or triple-product) coefficient that produced the final scale (e.g., the common correct factor 1/6 with an edge-based construction, or 1/3 when using a single-face base and a perpendicular distance to the opposite plane). The incorrect attempt failed mainly due to dimensional/structural confusion about how the two face altitudes combine (treating a product of lengths as if it were a length without the right separation argument), and especially due to an incorrect coefficient/derivation for the volume formula; this led to the final factor being too large.

items.title: Convert Tangency to Equal Distance Constraints
items.description: When a circle is tangent to lines, translate the geometry into equal perpendicular distances from the center to those lines.
items.content: Use the fact that tangency to a line means the radius equals the perpendicular distance from the center to the line. Then set up an equation “dist(center, line1) = dist(center, line2)” and solve for the center’s position. This is typically more reliable than reasoning about angle bisectors or segment ratios unless those are derived explicitly.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently used a robust, general method: coordinate placement for the trapezoid to compute line equations/length scales, then converted tangency into an equality of perpendicular distances from the circle’s center to the two tangent lines. They carefully used the fact that the circle’s center lies on the base, so distances simplify cleanly using the known side lengths (turning denominators into the leg lengths), and they verified assumptions like the sign/absolute value depending on which segment the center lies on. The incorrect attempts also aimed for the same overall approach, but failed by invoking a questionable “ratio” claim about angle bisectors/segment splitting, or by making unjustified simplifications about distance formulas (e.g., mishandling normalization factors/denominators when writing distance to a line, or substituting a “clean” denominator without proving it from the given geometry). This led to inconsistent results for the radius-distance expressions and hence for the tangency point along the base.

items.title: Use Coordinate Placement to Make Distances Cancel Nicely
items.description: Choose coordinates so the unknowns appear only in simple line equations, letting known side lengths simplify the distance denominators.
items.content: Place parallel lines horizontally and parametrize the legs with a height and a horizontal shift; the leg lengths then directly give the normalization factors in point-to-line distance formulas. This creates cancellations that avoid messy radicals and reduces the problem to a linear equation in the desired parameter.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently used a robust, general method: coordinate placement for the trapezoid to compute line equations/length scales, then converted tangency into an equality of perpendicular distances from the circle’s center to the two tangent lines. They carefully used the fact that the circle’s center lies on the base, so distances simplify cleanly using the known side lengths (turning denominators into the leg lengths), and they verified assumptions like the sign/absolute value depending on which segment the center lies on. The incorrect attempts also aimed for the same overall approach, but failed by invoking a questionable “ratio” claim about angle bisectors/segment splitting, or by making unjustified simplifications about distance formulas (e.g., mishandling normalization factors/denominators when writing distance to a line, or substituting a “clean” denominator without proving it from the given geometry). This led to inconsistent results for the radius-distance expressions and hence for the tangency point along the base.

items.title: Validate Absolute Values and Sign Assumptions
items.description: When using distance formulas, justify how absolute values resolve based on the center’s location.
items.content: Distance to a line involves absolute values; you must determine which side of the line the point lies on (e.g., “between endpoints” on a segment) so the absolute value can be replaced by a correct expression. Unchecked sign assumptions are a common source of incorrect final algebra.

items.title: Translate Complex Constraints into Concrete Coordinate Inequalities
items.description: When conditions constrain real and imaginary parts of expressions, first rewrite them as explicit bounds on Cartesian coordinates.
items.content: Let \(z=x+iy\) and convert “real and imaginary parts between \(a\) and \(b\)” into inequalities on \(x\) and \(y\). For expressions like \(z/\overline{z}\) or \(1/\overline{z}\), rationalize to get real/imag parts as explicit rational functions of \(x,y\), then convert the interval bounds into inequalities on those functions. This avoids ambiguity about how the complex constraint shapes the region.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently: (1) converted the algebraic “real/imag parts in an interval” constraints into explicit inequalities on Cartesian coordinates; (2) carefully handled the second constraint via rationalization to get two inequalities that become outside-of-circle conditions; and (3) computed the area using geometry (either inclusion–exclusion with correctly identified overlap, or polar-coordinate radial bounds) while respecting that the first constraint restricts to a square in the first quadrant. Unsuccessful attempts typically failed in one of two ways: confusing “outside both circles” with “inside either circle,” or mishandling the circle pieces inside the bounded square (e.g., incorrectly treating excluded regions as full circles or miscomputing overlap/lens contributions), which led to impossible negative areas. Another failure mode was using an overly convenient symmetry/sector assumption without verifying that the relevant portion of each circle actually lies in the square.

items.title: Rationalize Then Complete the Square to Reveal Geometric Region
items.description: Turn rational inequalities into standard geometric shapes by rationalizing and completing squares.
items.content: After rationalizing expressions involving \(\overline{z}\), inequalities of the form \( \frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\le \text{const}\) typically become quadratic inequalities in \(x,y\). Completing squares often reveals “inside a circle” vs “outside a circle” conditions, which is crucial for correct region characterization. Verify the direction of the inequality after rearrangement to prevent inverted “forbidden vs allowed” sets.

items.title: Compute Area via Inclusion–Exclusion Only After Correctly Identifying Forbidden/Allowed Sets
items.description: Use inclusion–exclusion to subtract unions, but only after determining whether you must subtract circles, add overlaps, or both.
items.content: Once the region is expressed as the intersection of bounded domain (like a square) with “outside/inside” constraints, determine whether the forbidden region is a union of disk parts or the allowed region is a complement. Inclusion–exclusion should match that logic: if allowed points are outside both disks, then the excluded union of disk portions should be subtracted, with overlap added back. A quick sanity check (e.g., area can’t be negative) helps catch mis-signed inclusion–exclusion.

items.title: Respect Domain Truncation When Using Circle Areas
items.description: When circles are intersected with a bounded region, compute only the portion that lies inside the bounds; don’t assume full circles or whole overlaps.
items.content: Even if circle equations are simple, the actual region of interest is often a circle clipped by another constraint (like a square in the first quadrant). Successful solutions either (a) use geometry to argue the exact portion (quarter/semicircle/sector) that lies in the square, or (b) avoid piecewise geometry by using polar bounds that automatically enforce truncation. Many failures came from treating pieces as “full” when only halves/quarters lie in the domain.

items.title: Sanity-Check Geometry with Impossibility Tests
items.description: If your computed area becomes negative or wildly inconsistent, re-check region direction and overlap handling.
items.content: Negative area is a strong indicator of an inequality direction error or an inclusion–exclusion sign mistake (e.g., swapping “outside” and “inside” or miscomputing overlap). Similarly, large discrepancies often signal that the chosen overlap corresponds to the full circles rather than the truncated portions within the bounded domain. Use these checks early to correct the region model before final arithmetic.

items.title: Compose Reflections Using Angle Algebra
items.description: When reflecting lines through the origin, convert each reflection into an angle formula and algebraically compose them to get the net transformation.
items.content: Represent each line by its direction angle and use the reflection rule “angle → 2α − angle” with α the reflecting line’s angle. Apply the rule sequentially for each reflection, then simplify; this prevents mistakes that come from trying to track slopes/coordinates directly across multiple reflections.
items.reasoning: All successful attempts used the same robust approach: model reflections on lines through the origin as transformations on an “angle parameter,” compose the two reflections to get a simple net action (a constant angle shift), then determine the order by solving a modular condition that accounts for the fact that a line has no direction (angles differing by π represent the same line). The incorrect attempt included essentially the same correct logic, so the “incorrect” label likely reflects a mismatch with the platform’s checking or a transcription issue rather than a genuine reasoning flaw. Comparing across attempts still highlights the key transferable distinctions: correctly composing reflections, choosing the right modulus (π for lines), and justifying why the initial line’s slope is irrelevant because the transformation acts uniformly.

items.title: Account for Line Indistinguishability (Mod π)
items.description: For lines through the origin, equality is determined modulo π because opposite directions describe the same line.
items.content: When you test whether a transformation returns a line to itself, impose the condition that the angle change is a multiple of π (not 2π). This avoids off-by-a-factor-of-two errors common when working with angles while ignoring that a “line” has no orientation.

items.title: Encode States via a Structural Invariant
items.description: Find and justify an invariant describing the allowed form of every reachable state, then count those forms.
items.content: When a game move has a “remove a quadrant” flavor, the remaining configuration often becomes monotone/closed under a partial order (e.g., Ferrers/Young diagram). Prove this property explicitly, then use it as the basis for a state-counting model; this prevents counting arbitrary shapes that are not actually reachable.
items.reasoning: All “correct” attempts converged on the same robust invariant: after any moves, the remaining cells must have a Ferrers/Young-diagram monotonicity property, so game states correspond bijectively to monotone boundary descriptions (lattice paths or nonincreasing row-length sequences). The unsuccessful attempt matches the correct characterization and counting too; the provided “Incorrect” is thus likely due to a hidden issue not reflected in the text (e.g., a presentation/justification gap). Across attempts, success correlated with (1) explicitly proving the invariant shape property, (2) using a bijective encoding that is complete (counts all states) and sound (counts only reachable states), and (3) selecting a standard, verifiable enumeration model (stars-and-bars / lattice paths) with correct parameters.

items.title: Use a Bijection-Based Counting Model
items.description: Count combinatorial objects by switching representations only after establishing a one-to-one correspondence.
items.content: A successful approach maps each valid state to a unique representation (like a monotone lattice path or a nonincreasing sequence), and also argues that every such representation corresponds to a valid state. This “both directions” check prevents subtle overcounting/undercounting that can arise when the mapping is only suggestive.

items.title: Prefer Standard Enumerations with Parameter Checks
items.description: Once you have a monotone encoding, use a known counting formula and verify the parameters match the board dimensions.
items.content: For lattice-path or monotone-sequence encodings, ensure the counts use the correct step numbers/sequence length and bounds implied by the grid size. Doing this carefully turns a potentially ambiguous counting problem into a direct application of combinations-with-repetition or binomial path counts.

items.title: Parameterize Ratios with a Single Scale Variable
items.description: When a problem fixes only a side ratio, rewrite both sides as a common scale times their ratio components so all constraints become equations in just that scale and one geometric parameter.
items.content: This collapses the degrees of freedom and makes subsequent use of Law of Cosines and area formulas straightforward. It also prevents common mistakes where one tries to optimize while treating the ratio sides as independently variable.
items.reasoning:
Successful attempts consistently: (1) parameterized the constrained side ratio explicitly with a single scale variable, (2) expressed area via a two-sides-and-included-angle formula, then (3) used the Law of Cosines to link that included angle to the fixed third side, producing a one-variable optimization over an angle (or its cosine). They then either used calculus/standard maximization to find the critical point and verified it lies in the feasible range, yielding a clean exact maximum.
Unsuccessful attempts typically: (a) assumed the area is maximized at a right angle without proving feasibility globally under the constraint, (b) forced a right-angle condition in the wrong place or for an angle not independently free, or (c) tried to appeal to “AIME/integer-ness” to justify incorrect maximizers instead of deriving the true maximum. Another failure mode was doing algebraic optimization but mishandling the optimization variable (mixing constraints, using the wrong angle, or producing an expression that doesn’t respect triangle feasibility), leading to non-global or incorrect maxima.

items.title: Couple Area and Constraints Using Two Sides + Included Angle
items.description: Express area as (product of two sides)·sin(included angle)/2, then use the Law of Cosines to express that included angle (or its cosine) in terms of the fixed side constraints.
items.content: This yields an explicit objective function of a single variable (typically cos or the angle). The optimization then becomes well-posed and avoids guessing which angle configuration maximizes area.

items.title: Optimize the Correct Scalar Objective (Often After Eliminating the Scale)

items.description: If you consider special cases like a right angle to simplify maximization, you must verify that that configuration is compatible with all constraints (including the fixed third side) and is not on the degeneracy boundary.

items.title: Don’t Let Output Formatting Drive the Math
items.description: Formatting expectations (e.g., “answers should be integers”) should never replace the real maximization/feasibility analysis; instead, re-derive the maximum exactly from the constraints.
items.content: Unsuccessful drafts started doubting results because of non-integer fractions, leading to unjustified changes to the maximizing condition. The correct approach is to treat those formatting notes as downstream verification, not as a mathematical constraint.

items.title: Derive the Concurrency Relation for Your Exact Variables
items.description: When using “standard” formulas for concurrent cevians, re-derive (or at least verify) the relation in terms of your exact ratio definitions rather than trusting a memorized identity.
items.content: Many wrong attempts used a concurrency identity that corresponds to a different choice of ratios (or different point/segment orientation), producing inconsistent equations. The successful approach tied the ratios to barycentric/mass-point structure and only then extracted the correct symmetric relations. A quick sanity check—confirming the identity holds under basic substitutions or by re-deriving key steps—prevents this mismatch.
items.reasoning: The successful attempt managed to (1) introduce auxiliary variables representing the target ratios, (2) model the concurrency point with barycentric/mass-point parameters that correctly connect those ratios to geometric side ratios, and (3) derive the needed symmetric relations carefully from those correct structural equations—then used the given sum to compute the product. In contrast, the incorrect attempts repeatedly applied “standard identities” about concurrent cevians but without verifying the prerequisites or deriving the specific identity matching the chosen definition of variables (e.g., confusing segment-splitting ratios with mass-point masses, or using formulas for different but similar configurations). Many also expanded algebraic expressions (like products of (1+x)) but paired them with an incompatible concurrency constraint, leading to contradictions, nonphysical results (negative/zero), or wildly inconsistent products. A common failure mode was overreliance on assumed identities (like xyz equals a linear combination of x+y+z) without a derivation check.

items.title: Use Symmetric Polynomials to Close the System
items.description: After converting geometry to algebra with variables, express the goal through symmetric sums (x+y+z, xy+yz+zx, xyz) so a small set of derived identities determines the product uniquely.
items.content: The correct solution first reduced the given information to x+y+z and then used concurrency to obtain a second symmetric relation, allowing xyz to be determined. Wrong attempts often left the system underconstrained or derived expansions (like (1+x)(1+y)(1+z)) but then plugged in an unrelated constraint, failing to consistently determine all symmetric terms.

items.description: When concurrency is present, model the intersection point with barycentric or mass-point weights to systematically translate geometric constraints into algebraic equations.
items.content: Consecutive “standard identity” usage was unreliable in failures, but the successful attempt employed barycentric coordinates/mass points to connect segment ratios to underlying weights. This approach is robust: once the weight model is set up, algebra follows mechanically and reduces the chance of using an incorrect identity for the wrong configuration.

items.title: Count Missing Values via Jump-Size Skips
items.description: For monotone integer-valued “step” functions, missing outputs occur exactly where the step jump is larger than 1, and a jump of size t skips exactly t−1 values.
items.content: When you can express an output as a sum of floor terms or a valuation-based function, check how the function changes between consecutive inputs. If the increase at a step is t>1, then the intermediate integers are unattained; thus the total number of missing outputs up to a threshold becomes a sum of (t−1) over the relevant steps. This turns a hard “which values are attainable?” question into systematic counting of events causing large jumps.
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts (1,2,3,4,5,6) share a coherent core: model trailing zeros as a monotone step function of an integer parameter, identify that “missing” values arise exactly when the step jump exceeds 1, and then count those skipped values via a structured summation/counting of how many times large 5-adic contributions occur before the target threshold. They also correctly determine the stopping point (the smallest parameter where the function reaches the target level) so that only skips producing values below the bound are counted.

The incorrect attempts typically fail in one of two ways: (1) they apply a remembered but mismatched closed-form formula (e.g., giving too small a number like 97 or wrong constants like 366/319), often with off-by-one or wrong threshold mapping (confusing “<1992” vs “≤1991” and whether the count includes the 0 value), or (2) they reason qualitatively about skipped values but lose the rigorous link between “jump size” and the exact summation they compute—leading to an incorrect counting range or an incorrect summand structure.

items.title: Translate Valuation Events into Counting Multiples
items.description: Convert the cause of a large jump (e.g., extra p-adic factors) into divisibility conditions, then count those events with floor-division.
items.content: If a function’s increment equals an exponent v_p(something), then “jump ≥2” corresponds to divisibility by p^2, “jump ≥3” to divisibility by p^3, etc. The contribution of each level can be counted by the number of integers divisible by p^k in a range, computed as floor(range/p^k). This yields robust, checkable summations without needing to enumerate the function’s outputs.

items.title: Fix the Threshold by Finding the First Parameter Hitting the Level
items.description: When counting skipped values below a bound, locate the smallest input where the monotone function reaches (or exceeds) the bound, so your counting range matches exactly the outputs you care about.
items.content: Monotonicity ensures that once the function passes the target output level, any later skips affect outputs at or above the bound. Therefore, determine the minimal input M where the function reaches the threshold (e.g., ≥B), then count only jump-causing events up to M that can produce skipped outputs <B. This prevents off-by-one and range errors that plague many incorrect approaches.

items.title: Do an Explicit Off-by-One Audit on Bounds and Positivity
items.description: Always reconcile whether your count includes the endpoint value, and whether special cases like n=0 are excluded, before finalizing a formula.
items.content: Many failures come from mismatching “n<bound” with “n≤bound−1” and from forgetting that the function may attain n=0 for small inputs. Before computing, decide the exact set of integers being counted (e.g., positive n only, and strict vs non-strict inequalities) and verify how your counting method maps to that set. A quick boundary check can correct incorrect closed-form uses.

items.title: Model the Constraints by Position Before Counting
items.description: Translate every condition (range, parity, distinct digits) into explicit allowed sets for each digit position, then count systematically.
items.content: Successful solutions first identify what the range implies for the leading digit, what parity implies for the last digit, and what “all digits different” implies across all positions. items.title: Parametrize using the linear constraint to simplify the quadratic

items.content: In problems with both a linear and a quadratic/determinant-like condition, first use the linear equation to rewrite one variable in terms of others or switch to difference variables. A good parameter choice often turns the quadratic condition into a product (e.g., “difference × difference = constant”), shrinking the search space to factor pairs. This avoids messy algebra and reduces the likelihood of sign/order errors.

The unsuccessful attempts typically failed by (1) introducing variables but enforcing the “adjacent difference” relations incorrectly (missing or mis-setting which differences must be equal), or (2) concluding extra factor-pair cases that don’t satisfy the strict order constraints, thereby overcounting. items.title: Use Symmetry to Uniformize Sequential Sampling
items.description: When draws happen in stages without replacement, re-express the process as choosing a larger set at once and then randomly partitioning it, so all configurations become uniformly likely.
items.content: Check whether “draw group A, then group B from the remainder” can be modeled as: choose 6 distinct elements uniformly, then choose which 3 become A uniformly among the 6. This prevents subtle probability bugs that arise from treating the split as “smallest 3 vs largest 3” rather than the actual random partition induced by the two-stage sampling.

The failures come from incorrect translation of the prefix/ordering condition (e.g., flipping the inequality direction or counting patterns using an incomplete necessary condition), or from asserting an intermediate “standard result” without correctly matching the exact event being counted. items.title: Translate the process into cumulative modular displacement
items.description: Convert iterative labeling rules into a single expression for the total clockwise move from the start, then use modular arithmetic to test when two labels coincide.
items.content: When a construction repeatedly moves around a cyclic structure, define the “state” as a position index modulo the cycle length. Sum the step sizes dictated by the rule to get a closed-form displacement to label n, then express “same point” as congruence of displacements. This avoids getting lost in the step-by-step evolution and makes collision conditions precise.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently model the process as modular “displacement” on a fixed-size circular state space, deriving a closed-form formula for the cumulative clockwise move from the starting label to any other label. They then translate “same point” into a congruence condition on these cumulative moves, and finally seek the *smallest* positive label satisfying that congruence, which often reduces to solving a structured quadratic/truncated congruence. The incorrect attempts typically fail by mis-indexing the step sizes (using the wrong summation range, off-by-one errors like summing to the wrong endpoint, or confusing “count k points” with “move k steps”), or by concluding a congruence solution directly without properly checking the computed residue and/or without justifying minimality. One incorrect attempt also reduces modulo incorrectly (getting the wrong residue for the displacement), causing the entire collision search to drift.

items.title: Use Double-Counting to Build a Closed System
items.description: Derive multiple independent equations by counting the same structure (edges, corners, face-vertex incidences) in two ways.
items.content: When you have polyhedra/graphs with face types and uniform local structure, count a global quantity (like total edge incidences or total face-vertex incidences) in two distinct ways to get equations relating the unknowns. Then use Euler’s formula as the third relation to close the system. This prevents relying on unstated geometric assumptions.
items.reasoning: Most attempts succeed by building a consistent “incidence-counting + Euler” system, then solving the resulting integer constraints. The successful approaches keep the variables conceptually separated (face counts vs. per-vertex counts) and use only robust combinatorial equalities (edge/face incidence, face/vertex incidence, Euler’s formula). The incorrect attempt mostly succeeds too, but it contains a key risk: it asserts that “corner totals” can be equated in a way that implicitly assumes all corners correspond cleanly to face-sides without justifying the mapping carefully; similarly, it relies on setting up equations like 3x=VT that are correct only if the per-vertex quantities truly match the same triangle-face incidence counting. The safer successful attempts either (a) derive the same equations from two independent incidence counts without mixing meanings, or (b) explicitly eliminate intermediate face-count variables to reach a clean linear Diophantine equation (e.g., 3T+5P=16) and then use integrality/feasibility to pick the unique solution.

items.title: Keep “Per-face” and “Per-vertex” Variables Separate
items.description: Avoid algebra mistakes by clearly distinguishing counts of faces from counts of how many faces meet at a vertex.
items.content: Label variables by what they count (e.g., number of faces of each type vs. number of incident face types at each vertex) and write incidence equations that match those interpretations exactly. Successful attempts consistently used equations like 3*(#triangular faces)=V*(#triangles at a vertex) and similarly for pentagons, which only works if the meanings are tracked carefully.

items.title: Compute Conditional Win Probabilities from the First-Event Structure
items.description: Before modeling multiple rounds, derive the win probability given who starts by using the distribution of the first decisive event (e.g., first success on odd/even turns).
items.content: When a game ends at the first occurrence of an event, the outcome is determined by where that event happens (often indexed by turn parity). Compute win probabilities conditioned on the starter by summing over the possible first-event positions, rather than assuming each player has a uniform 1/2 chance.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all correctly identify the single-game winning probabilities based on the parity/position of the first “success” event (the first head), then propagate this through a state variable representing who goes first in the next game. The common reason failures occurred is assuming symmetry too aggressively (e.g., treating winner probabilities as 1/2 regardless of starter) or mishandling the “roles swap/next starter depends on loser” dynamics, leading to incorrect transition recurrences and therefore wrong final fractions. Another distinction is that the successful solutions either iteratively compute the starter probability with a validated recurrence or solve the induced two-state Markov process cleanly; unsuccessful ones often introduce an equation that doesn’t properly reflect whose probability (current player vs next player) is being conditioned on.

items.title: Switch to a Verified Tangency Condition
items.description: Model visibility changes as tangency events and enforce tangency with a robust invariant rather than ad-hoc intersection reasoning.
items.content: When a segment “just begins to be blocked/unblocked,” treat it as tangent to the obstacle boundary. A reliable method is to convert tangency into an invariant like “distance from the circle center to the line equals the radius,” then solve using that condition. This avoids mistakes from incorrectly assuming where the line of sight intersects or “touches” at specific y-levels.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts (1 and 2) share a consistent geometry-to-algebra pipeline: (i) model the building as a circle tangent to the evolving line of sight; (ii) determine the “first blocking” configuration using tangency and the given separation constraint; (iii) parametrize both walkers’ positions over time; (iv) write the line through their positions and enforce tangency via a clean invariant (distance from origin to a line equals radius) and solve for the second tangency. Unsuccessful attempts fail in predictable ways: they misinterpret what “200 feet apart” constrains (Euclidean vs along-motion separation), they assume an incorrect initial tangency (e.g., vertical line tangent when circle radius makes it impossible), or they mishandle the tangency condition when squaring/absolute values (leading to sign errors and picking the wrong root). Attempt 3 and 5 show “got negative/irrational time” symptoms that weren’t debugged by revisiting the modeling assumptions (interpretation of the given 200, or which tangency corresponds to “see again”). Overall, success correlates with careful constraint formalization and validating tangency conditions against feasibility before solving.

items.title: Be Careful with Squaring and Absolute Values
items.description: When tangency equations involve absolute values or squaring, explicitly track signs to pick the physically correct root.

Then compute distances between two tangency points by subtracting these adjacent tangency lengths along the same segment. This avoids common failures where one incorrectly assumes the tangency point is a fixed inradius distance from an endpoint.

items.title: Disambiguate Radii and Tangency Direction
items.description: Convert all textual circle information into unambiguous radii and center-distance equations before doing geometry.
items.content: When a problem mentions internal tangency and also gives a diameter/radius, first write the radius of each circle explicitly and determine which one contains the other. Then use the internal tangency rule to set the distance between centers as the difference of radii, not the sum. This prevents cascading coordinate and tangent-line errors.
items.reasoning: The correct solution attempts share a disciplined approach: (1) clarify which circle has which radius from the diameter and “internally tangent” wording, (2) set up a consistent coordinate/placement where tangency implies a fixed line of centers and a fixed tangent line direction at the tangency point, and (3) translate the square condition into an explicit geometric constraint (a side of the square lying on a tangent line, with the other vertices constrained by lying on the larger circle). Incorrect attempts most often fail by mixing up which radius belongs to which circle, or by making an unjustified assumption about square placement (e.g., assuming a tangency point is the midpoint of a side without deriving it from symmetry/constraints). items.title: Convert Geometry to Deterministic Algebra Early
items.description: Turn triangle/altitude information into exact formulas relating the given length to the sides and to the cosine, before applying number theory.
items.content: In right-triangle altitude problems, write the standard projection relations immediately (e.g., express the given segment as a rational function of the hypotenuse and a leg). Then link the cosine to the same side ratio (cosine = leg/hypotenuse) so the “given” translates into a strict equation that can be attacked with divisibility and squareness. This prevents later reasoning from being based on unjustified pattern guesses.
items.reasoning: The successful solutions (Attempts 1,2) reliably encoded the right-triangle geometry into exact algebraic relations between the given segment (the altitude projection) and the sides, then used integrality/rationality constraints to restrict possible numerator/denominator patterns for the cosine. They made the key move of eliminating unnecessary unknowns and converting “cosine is a reduced fraction” into divisibility/square conditions that are easy to finish with factor pairs. The incorrect attempts often either (a) guessed extra structure from the prime-power nature without fully justifying the divisibility/squareness implications, (b) mixed up which altitude relations apply (leading to inconsistent equations), or (c) introduced parameters but failed to validate that the resulting candidates actually produce the required integer altitude segment. A repeated failure mode was trying to infer the cosine directly by “forcing” hypotenuse/leg values, without doing the square/perfect-square check on the remaining side.

items.title: Use Rational-Reduced Cosine to Impose Divisibility/Square Constraints
items.description: When a quantity is given as a reduced rational, translate that reduction into gcd-based divisibility and perfect-square requirements.
items.content: Let cos B = m/n in lowest terms and rewrite the geometric equation to isolate expressions like m² | (prime power) and n² | (something). With gcd(m,n)=1, you can often deduce which primes can appear in m and force m to be a limited set. After narrowing m, enforce that the remaining side computed from Pythagorean relations is an integer by requiring a derived discriminant (e.g., n²−m²) to be a perfect square.

items.description: Prime-power data strongly suggests restrictions, but always finish by checking the perfect-square/integer condition for the remaining side(s).

items.title: Disentangle “At time t” vs “First hit by time T”
items.description: When asked for probability of reaching by a deadline, don’t just sum probabilities of being at the target at multiple times—account for trajectories that visit earlier and re-visit later.
items.content: Compute “at time t” counts easily via multinomial/dynamic counting, but for “hit by time ≤T” you must either use first-hit decomposition (e.g., first at time t) or subtract overlaps (paths that are at the target at later times after having already visited earlier). A common mistake is assuming “at time t1” and “at time t2” are disjoint events when the event is “reach by T,” not “be at the target at exactly one time.”
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts all share the same reliable structure: (1) identify which step counts can possibly reach the target using basic invariants like Manhattan distance and parity, (2) compute probabilities for being at the target at those specific times by careful counting of direction-multinomial possibilities, and (3) convert “at time t” probabilities into “hit by time ≤T” via a correct double-counting/inclusion-exclusion step (usually handled by separating first-hit scenarios or subtracting paths that have already been there earlier).
The unsuccessful attempts fail in two main ways: they (a) add “being at the target at time 4” and “being at the target at time 6” while treating them as disjoint “reaches by time ≤6” without handling that time-6 paths may include trajectories that already visited at time 4, and (b) in some attempts, they conflate “exactly at time t” with “first reach time t,” effectively missing the necessary subtraction/conditioning.
A smaller failure mode appears where attempts propose feasible times but do not fully justify whether earlier times are impossible or whether “first hit” is being tracked correctly, leading to overcounting.

items.title: Use invariants to restrict candidate times before counting
items.description: Before doing any counting, use distance and parity (or other invariants) to sharply reduce which time steps can even reach the target.
items.content: For lattice-step random walks, Manhattan distance gives a hard lower bound on the earliest possible time, and parity constraints (e.g., of coordinate sums) determine whether reaching is possible at that time at all. This prevents wasted work and reduces the chance of logical mistakes about which times must be included.

items.title: Count endpoints with disciplined multinomial decomposition
items.description: To count probability of being at a specific lattice point after t steps, decompose into how many steps go in each coordinate direction and then use multinomial/binomial factors consistently.
items.content: Set up variables for counts of right/left and up/down steps with linear equations from the required net displacement, then count sequences by choosing positions of each type (multinomial coefficient). This approach is robust and helps avoid algebraic or double-counting errors that come from mixing conditional and unconditional probabilities.

items.title: Validate the overlap term via a short “return-to-state” calculation
items.description: If you subtract paths that were already at the target earlier, compute the required conditional probability of returning from that state over the remaining steps.
items.content: Once you know a later-time position implies the walk was at the target earlier, the “overlap” is the probability of returning to the same state in the remaining time. For short remaining horizons, enumerate the minimal step sequences that return to the origin/state to get an exact overlap factor.

items.title: Translate Tangency to Exact Distance Constraints
items.description: Convert “a line is tangent” statements into explicit perpendicular-distance equations to circle centers.
items.content: When a line is tangent to a circle, its perpendicular distance from the circle’s center equals the circle’s radius (with a controlled sign/side depending on which “external/internal” tangent is intended). Set up equations for the two circles simultaneously; this directly yields the tangent line’s geometry without relying on ambiguous interpretations of the chord.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converged on a consistent geometric model: place all circle centers in a collinear configuration dictated by internal/external tangency, then translate “common external tangent” into a precise condition on the tangent line’s perpendicular distances to the two smaller-circle centers (with careful sign choices). They then computed the tangent line’s distance from the large-circle center to get the chord length via the standard chord-distance relation. The unsuccessful attempts failed mainly by misinterpreting which “common external tangent” is meant relative to the chord segment (sometimes treating a tangent line as if it degenerated or using the wrong orientation), and/or by mixing signed-distance assumptions inconsistently, leading to contradictions (e.g., chord distance too large or impossible slope/sign equations). One wrong attempt succeeded in solving “tangent to both small circles” but chose an orientation that made the implied perpendicular distance from the large center incorrect; others failed by asserting a linear/homothety center fact without fully reconciling it with the tangency-distance equations.

items.title: Get Chord Length from Center-to-Line Distance
items.description: Once you know the distance from a circle’s center to the chord’s supporting line, chord length follows immediately.
items.content: For a circle of radius R, if the chord’s supporting line is at perpendicular distance p from the center, then chord length satisfies \(L=2\sqrt{R^2-p^2}\), so \(L^2=4(R^2-p^2)\). This provides a reliable final verification step: check that p is strictly less than R (otherwise the “chord” would degenerate).

items.description: “External tangent” often requires consistent sign/orientation in signed-distance or coordinate equations; validate the configuration.

Keep a clear convention for which side of each center the tangent line lies on, and confirm it yields a plausible perpendicular distance from the large-circle center (not exceeding its radius).

items.title: Use Complement Pairing on the Full Set, Then Subtract
items.description: Apply the involution pairing (d ↔ total/divisor) to the entire divisor set first, then handle extra constraints by subtraction.
items.content: When a strict inequality like d < N appears alongside divisor counting, pair each divisor with its complement (e.g., N²/d) to convert the inequality into “exactly one from each pair.” This works cleanly on the full divisor set because equality points are identifiable; after that, restrict further constraints (like “also divides N”) by subtracting their count from the already-validated pool.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently use a reliable divisor-pairing/complement-counting strategy with a strict inequality, then subtract the subset that also satisfies the divisibility condition. The unsuccessful attempt(s) fail by incorrectly applying “half” symmetry to a restricted subset (“bad” divisors that do not divide the original number) without proving that the restriction is preserved under the pairing map; this leads to parity/arithmetic contradictions and then an overly complicated direct case split that departs from the proven method. items.title: Exploit Symmetry with an Axis-Aligned Coordinate Model
items.description: When a problem is symmetric about an altitude/median, place coordinates so that symmetry becomes a reflection across an axis, making both points and angles easy to parameterize.
items.content: Align the coordinate system with the altitude to the base so the base is horizontal and the isosceles triangle is symmetric about the altitude axis. This typically turns unknown geometry into one parameter (base half-length or a tangent), enabling direct computation of both side lengths and angle relationships.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a clean, consistent pipeline: (1) exploit symmetry to choose coordinates aligned with the altitude, (2) convert the angle condition into a solvable trigonometric equation using half-angle/tangent forms, and (3) only then compute lengths and the perimeter. The incorrect attempts mostly fail in transferring between angle measures and tangent parameters—either by setting up a coordinate system where the “angles with an axis/vertical” correspond incorrectly to half-angles, or by introducing a more complicated expression for the perimeter (e.g., using sec/tan transforms) without keeping the angle-geometry mapping rigorously consistent, leading to inconsistent or non-simplifying equations.

Successful solutions consistently ensure the chosen tangent variable corresponds exactly to the geometric half-angle being used.

This prevents invalid conclusions where “unchanged sides” are assumed to be “unchanged under correspondence.”

items.description: When a function rounds to the nearest integer, convert it into inequalities using the midpoints (half-integers) and then count integers inside that open/closed interval.

After transforming via a monotone function (e.g., raising to a power), count integers in the resulting range using floors/ceils. This prevents off-by-one errors that come from guessing bucket endpoints directly.

items.title: Choose Minimal Sufficient Run-Suffix States
items.description: Model the process with states that encode only the run-length information that can affect which stopping condition happens next.
items.content: For “first to pattern A or pattern B” problems, track the shortest suffix/run summary that determines whether you’re one step from either termination (e.g., run lengths capped just below the win/lose thresholds). This prevents missing dependencies while keeping the state space small enough to solve exactly.

items.title: Write Correct One-Step Recurrences with Absorbing Boundaries
items.description: Turn the Markov chain into a solvable linear system by expressing each state’s success probability as an average of the next-state probabilities, with strict absorbing values.
items.content: For every transient state, set up equations of the form “success probability = (1/2)·(success from next state after H) + (1/2)·(success from next state after T),” and explicitly plug in 1 for success-absorbing states and 0 for failure-absorbing states. items.title: Use Light-Ray Projection with Similarity
items.description: Model the shadow boundary by projecting key “blocking” points (vertices/edges) from the light source onto the target plane and using similar triangles to get the scale factor.
items.content: When a point source casts a shadow from a solid onto a plane, the boundary of the shadow is determined by rays that pass through the object’s extreme features relative to the light. Pick those boundary-defining vertices/edges, write the line from the light to the feature, intersect it with the target plane, and use the resulting linear ratio (often from similar triangles) to compute the shadow’s dimensions.

items.title: Factor to a Product Equation, Then Count Divisor Pairs
items.description: When a Diophantine equation can be algebraically rearranged into a product equaling a fixed square, count solutions by translating to factor pairs of that square.
items.content: Use a substitution that turns the original relation into (linear-in-x)(linear-in-y)=constant. After the bijection between factor pairs and solution pairs is established, count by divisor enumeration, and reduce to counting unordered pairs with careful x<y handling. This prevents ad-hoc guessing and makes the counting space explicit.

- then reduced the counting to divisor pairs of a known number,
- and, crucially, handled the parity/integrality condition by requiring the factors used in the substitution to have specific 2-adic exponents (not just “even somewhere”),
- and finally corrected for the x<y constraint by subtracting the x=y (factor-square) fixed point before halving.

Unsuccessful attempts typically failed in one (or more) of these:
- parity/integrality constraints were treated too loosely (e.g., assuming “if one factor is even then the other is automatically even” without checking that the half-sum expressions remain integers),

- or they used an over-simplified “half the divisors” argument that ignores when the mapping between divisor pairs and ordered (x,y) is not perfectly symmetric due to integrality restrictions.

In factor-counting terms, this becomes constraints on the exponent of the prime 2 inside the divisor; verify the complementary factor’s parity exponent supports the required integrality. This avoids the common error of counting “even divisors” too broadly.
